title: EXPOSÉ XX REDUCTIVE GROUPS OF SEMISIMPLE RANK 1

pages.title: EXPOSÉ XX REDUCTIVE GROUPS OF SEMISIMPLE RANK 1

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XX
REDUCTIVE GROUPS OF SEMISIMPLE RANK 1by M. Demazure
1. Elementary systems. The groups \(\mathrm{U}_\alpha\) and \(\mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}\)

blocks.title: Reminder
blocks.html:
Reminder 1.1

Let \(\mathrm{S} = \mathrm{Spec}(k)\), where \(k\) is an algebraically closed field, and let G be a reductive S-group of semisimple rank 1 and T a (trivial) maximal torus of G. We then have \[ \mathfrak{g} = \mathfrak{t} \oplus \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \oplus \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}, \] where \(\alpha\) and \(-\alpha\) are the roots of G with respect to T. Moreover, there exist two group monomorphisms \[ p_\alpha : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \qquad \text{and} \qquad p_{-\alpha} : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] such that \[ t\, p_\alpha(x)\, t^{-1} = p_\alpha(\alpha(t)x) \qquad \text{and} \qquad t\, p_{-\alpha}(x)\, t^{-1} = p_{-\alpha}(\alpha(t)^{-1} x), \] for every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and all \(t \in \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(x \in \mathbb{G}_a(\mathrm{S}')\), and such that the morphism \[ \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{T} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}, \] defined by \((y, t, x) \mapsto p_{-\alpha}(y)\, t\, p_\alpha(x)\), is radicial and dominant (BIBLE, 13-12, cor. 2 to th. 3).
Since the tangent map at the identity element is bijective, this morphism is also separable, hence birational; by the Main Theorem (EGA III1, 4.4.9), it is therefore an open immersion.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, T a torus of G, Q a subtorus of T, and \(\alpha\) a character of T inducing on Q a character which is nontrivial on each fiber. Let \(p_\alpha : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{G}\) (resp. \(p_{-\alpha} : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{G}\)) be a group morphism normalized by T with multiplier \(\alpha\) (resp. \(-\alpha\)). Suppose that the morphism \[ u : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{T} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] defined set-theoretically by \(u(y, t, x) = p_{-\alpha}(y)\, t\, p_\alpha(x)\) is an open immersion. Finally, let \(q\) be an integer \(\geqslant 0\) and \[ p : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] a group morphism such that for every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and all \(t \in \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(x \in \mathbb{G}_a(\mathrm{S}')\) we have \[ \mathrm{int}(t)^q\big(p(x)\big) = p(\alpha(t)x). \] Then there exists a unique \(a \in \mathbb{G}_a(\mathrm{S})\) with \(p(x) = p_\alpha(a\, x^q)\).

Indeed, let \(\Omega\) be the image of \(u\) and \(\mathrm{U} = p^{-1}(\Omega)\). This is an open subscheme of \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) containing the zero section. For every section \(t\) of Q, the automorphism of \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) defined by multiplication by \(\alpha(t)\) leaves U globally fixed. We have \(\mathrm{U} = \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\); indeed, it suffices to check this when S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field \(k\); then \(\alpha : \mathrm{Q}(k) \to k^*\) is surjective, which immediately shows \(\mathrm{U}(k) \supset k^*\), hence \(\mathrm{U} = \mathbb{G}_{a,k}\). There therefore exist three morphisms \[ a : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}, \qquad b : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{T}, \qquad c : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}, \] with \(p(x) = p_{-\alpha}(a(x))\, b(x)\, p_\alpha(c(x))\). The condition on \(p\) translates into \[ a(\alpha(t)x) = \alpha(t)^{-q} a(x), \qquad b(\alpha(t)x) = b(x), \qquad c(\alpha(t)x) = \alpha(t)^q c(x). \] For the same reason as before, we therefore have, for every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and every \(z \in \mathbb{G}_m(\mathrm{S}')\), \[ a(zx) = z^{-q} a(x), \qquad b(zx) = b(x), \qquad c(zx) = z^q c(x), \] hence \[ z^q a(z) = a(1), \qquad b(z) = b(1), \qquad c(z) = z^q c(1). \] Since \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\) is schematically dense in \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\), we immediately get, for every \(x \in \mathbb{G}_a(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\): \[ \begin{aligned} &x^q a(x) = a(1) = \mathrm{Cte} = 0, && \text{whence } a = 0, \\ &b(x) = b(1) = \mathrm{Cte} = e, && \text{whence } b = e, \\ &c(x) = x^q c(1) = a x^q, && \text{for some } a \in \mathbb{G}_a(\mathrm{S}), \end{aligned} \] which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.3

Let S be a prescheme. An S-elementary system is a triple \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) where

  1. (i) G is a reductive S-group of semisimple rank 1 (Exp. XIX 2.7),
  2. (ii) T is a maximal torus of G,
  3. (iii) \(\alpha\) is a root of G with respect to T (Exp. XIX 3.2).

We therefore have a direct sum decomposition (Exp. XIX 3.5) \[ \mathfrak{g} = \mathfrak{t} \oplus \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \oplus \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}, \] with \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) and \(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\) locally free of rank one.

If \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) is an S-elementary system, then \((\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}, \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}'}, \alpha_{\mathrm{S}'})\) is an S′-elementary system for every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\). If \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) is an S-elementary system, then so is \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, -\alpha)\).

If S is a prescheme, G a reductive S-group, T a maximal torus of G, and \(\alpha\) a root of G with respect to T, then (Exp. XIX 3.9), \((\mathrm{Z}_\alpha, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) is an S-elementary system.

Let \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) be an S-elementary system. The invertible Module \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) is canonically endowed with a T-Module structure. We therefore also have a T-module structure on the vector bundle \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)\). On the other hand, the inner automorphisms of T define on G a structure of group with group of operators T.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.5

Let \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) be an S-elementary system.

(i) There exists a unique morphism of groups with group of operators T \[ \exp : \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha) \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] which induces on Lie Algebras the canonical morphism \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha \to \mathfrak{g}\). In other words, \(\exp\) is the unique morphism satisfying the following conditions: For every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and every \(t \in \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{X}' \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)(\mathrm{S}')\), we have \[ \exp(\mathrm{X} + \mathrm{X}') = \exp(\mathrm{X}) \exp(\mathrm{X}'), \] \[ \mathrm{int}(t)\big(\exp(\mathrm{X})\big) = \exp(\alpha(t)\mathrm{X}), \] \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\exp)(\mathrm{X}) = \mathrm{X}. \]

(ii) If we likewise define (in the S-elementary system \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, -\alpha)\)) \[ \exp : \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}, \] the morphism \[ \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}) \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{T} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha) \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] defined set-theoretically by \((\mathrm{Y}, t, \mathrm{X}) \mapsto \exp(\mathrm{Y}) \cdot t \cdot \exp(\mathrm{X})\) is an open immersion.

Suppose we have proved the existence of the required morphisms \(\exp\), and let us prove the other assertions of the theorem. Let us first prove (ii). Since both sides are of finite presentation and flat over S, it suffices to do so when S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field (SGA 1, I 5.7 and VIII 5.5). So let \(\mathrm{S} = \mathrm{Spec}\, k\). Let \(\mathrm{X} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^*\), \(\mathrm{Y} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})^*\). It suffices to prove that the morphism \[ \mathbb{G}_{a,k} \underset{k}{\times} \mathrm{T} \underset{k}{\times} \mathbb{G}_{a,k} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] defined by \((y, t, x) \mapsto \exp(y\mathrm{Y})\, t\, \exp(x\mathrm{X})\) is an open immersion. Now by 1.1 and 1.2, there exist \(a, b \in k\) with \[ \exp(y\mathrm{Y}) = p_{-\alpha}(a y) \qquad \text{and} \qquad \exp(x\mathrm{X}) = p_\alpha(b x). \] Since \(\exp : \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}) \to \mathrm{G}\) induces a monomorphism on Lie algebras, we have \(a \neq 0\); likewise \(b \neq 0\), and we are reduced to 1.1.

The uniqueness of the morphism \(\exp\) can be proved locally on S; we then reduce to the case where \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) and \(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\) are free, and it only remains to apply 1.2 (with \(\mathrm{Q} = \mathrm{T}\) and \(q = 1\)).

It therefore remains to prove the existence of the required morphism \(\exp\). Let us first note that, by virtue of faithfully flat descent theory and the preceding uniqueness assertion, it suffices to prove this existence locally on S for the (fpqc) topology. By the usual arguments using finite presentation, we reduce to the case where S is noetherian, then to the case where it is noetherian local. By virtue of the preceding remark, we may therefore content ourselves with proving the existence of the sought morphism \(\exp\) when \(\mathrm{S} = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm{A})\), with A a complete noetherian local ring with algebraically closed residue field \(k\). Let then \(p_0 : \mathbb{G}_{a,k} \to \mathrm{G}_k\) be a monomorphism of \(k\)-groups normalized by \(\mathrm{T}_k\) with multiplier \(\alpha_0 = \alpha \otimes_\mathrm{A} k\) (such exist by 1.1). We know (1.1 and 1.2) that the corresponding morphism \(\mathrm{T}_k \cdot_{\alpha_0} \mathbb{G}_{a,k} \to \mathrm{G}_k\) is an immersion, hence in particular a monomorphism. Let us provisionally grant the two following lemmas:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.6

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group of finite presentation, T an S-torus, \(\alpha\) a character of T nontrivial on each fiber, and \(s_0\) a point of S. Let \[ f : \mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] be a morphism of S-groups such that \(f_{s_0}\) is a monomorphism and the restriction of \(f\) to T is a monomorphism. There exists an open neighborhood U of \(s_0\) such that \(f|\mathrm{U}\) is a monomorphism.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.7

Let A be a complete noetherian local ring with algebraically closed residue field \(k\), \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) an A-elementary system, and \(p_0 : \mathbb{G}_{a,k} \to \mathrm{G}_k\) a morphism of \(k\)-groups normalized by \(\mathrm{T}_k\) with multiplier \(\alpha \otimes_\mathrm{A} k\). There exists a group morphism \(p : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{A}} \to \mathrm{G}\) normalized by T with multiplier \(\alpha\).

Let \(p\) be the morphism whose existence is asserted by 1.7. Let \(f : \mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{G}\) be the corresponding morphism. It satisfies the hypotheses of 1.6, hence is a monomorphism; in particular \(p\) is a monomorphism. We then conclude by Exp. XIX 4.9.

Proof of 1.6. Denote by \(e : \mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) the unit section. Since \(f\) is unramified at \(e(s_0)\), it is unramified at \(e(s)\) for all \(s\) in an open neighborhood U of \(s_0\); \(f|\mathrm{U}\) is therefore unramified (Exp. X 3.5), hence its kernel \(\mathrm{Ker}(f)_{\mathrm{U}}\) is unramified over U. To prove that \(f|\mathrm{U}\) is a monomorphism, it therefore suffices to prove that \(\mathrm{Ker}(f)_{\mathrm{U}}\) is radicial over U, which is a set-theoretic question. We are thus reduced to proving:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.8

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field; let N be a normal subgroup of \(\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,k}\) (\(\alpha\) a nontrivial character of the torus T), étale over \(k\) and such that \(\mathrm{N} \cap \mathrm{T} = \{e\}\). Then \(\mathrm{N} = \{e\}\).

We have \(\mathrm{int}(t')(t, x) = (t, \alpha(t')x)\). If \((t, x)\) is a point of N with \(x \neq 0\), then \((t, zx)\) is also a point of N for \(z \in k^*\) and \((t, x)\) is not isolated, so N is not quasi-finite. We therefore have set-theoretically \(\mathrm{N} \subset \mathrm{T}\) and we are done.

Proof of 1.7. Let \(\mathfrak{m}\) be the radical of A and \(\mathrm{S}_n = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm{A}/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1})\), \(n \geqslant 0\). Let us first show, by induction on \(n\), that \(p_0\) can be extended for each \(n\) to a morphism of \(\mathrm{S}_n\)-groups \[ p_n : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}_n} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}_n} \] normalized by \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}_n}\) with multiplier \(\alpha_{\mathrm{S}_n}\), the \(p_n\) satisfying moreover the condition \(p_{n+1} \times_{\mathrm{S}_{n+1}} \mathrm{S}_n = p_n\). Let \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\). The morphism \(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}_n} \to \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}_n}\) defined by \(p_n\) is denoted \(f_n\). Let us grant the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.9

If \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) is a \(k\)-elementary system, \(k\) algebraically closed, and if \(p : \mathbb{G}_{a,k} \to \mathrm{G}\) is a monomorphism normalized by T with multiplier \(\alpha\), then \(\mathrm{H}^2(\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,k}, \mathfrak{g}) = 0\) (we let \(\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,k}\) act on \(\mathfrak{g}\) via the morphism \(\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,k} \to \mathrm{G}\) defined by \(p\), and the adjoint representation of G).

By virtue of Exp. III 2.8, \(f_n\) will extend to a morphism of \(\mathrm{S}_{n+1}\)-groups \[ f'_{n+1} : \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}_{n+1}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}_{n+1}}. \] Now \(f'_{n+1}|\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}_{n+1}}\) and the canonical immersion of \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}_{n+1}}\) into \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}_{n+1}}\) have the same restriction to \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}_n}\). By Exp. III 2.5, there exists an element \(g \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}_{n+1})\) such that \(g \times_{\mathrm{S}_{n+1}} \mathrm{S}_n = e\) and such that \(f_{n+1} = \mathrm{int}(g) \circ f'_{n+1}\) restricts to \(\mathrm{T}_{n+1}\) along the canonical immersion of \(\mathrm{T}_{n+1}\). Let \(p_{n+1}\) be the restriction of \(f_{n+1}\) to \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}_{n+1}}\). It is a morphism normalized by \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}_{n+1}}\) with multiplier \(\alpha_{\mathrm{S}_{n+1}}\), which extends \(p_n\). We have thus constructed a coherent system \((f_n)\) and we must now algebraize it. Now we have:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.10

Let A be a complete noetherian local ring, \(\mathfrak{m}\) its radical, \(\mathrm{S} = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm{A})\), \(\mathrm{S}_n = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm{A}/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1})\), T an S-torus, \(\alpha\) a nonzero character of T, and X an affine S-scheme on which T acts. Let T act on \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) via \(\alpha\). Let \(q\) be an integer \(\geqslant 0\), and let \((f_n)_{n \geqslant 0}\) be a coherent system of morphisms \[ f_n : \mathbb{G}^q_{a,\mathrm{S}_n} \longrightarrow \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{S}_n} \] of objects with operators \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}_n}\). There exists a unique morphism of objects with operators T \[ f : \mathbb{G}^q_{a,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{X} \] which induces the \(f_n\) (compare with Exp. IX 7.1).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.11

If X is a group with group of operators T and if the \(f_n\) are group morphisms, then \(f\) is one as well.
It suffices to apply the uniqueness assertion of the lemma to the two morphisms \(\mathbb{G}^{2q}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{X}\) deduced from \(f\) in the usual manner.

Proof of 1.10. Suppose T is trivial, which is moreover the case in the application of 1.10 to the proof of 1.5. We know (Exp. I 4.7.3, remark) that \(\mathrm{X} \mapsto \mathscr{A}(\mathrm{X})\) realizes an equivalence between the category of affine S-schemes endowed with an action of T and the category opposite to that of graded S-algebras of type \(\mathrm{M} = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\text{S-gr.}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}})\). We therefore have gradings \[ \mathrm{B} = \mathscr{A}(\mathrm{X}) = \coprod_{m \in \mathrm{M}} \mathrm{B}_m \qquad \text{and} \qquad \mathrm{C} = \mathscr{A}(\mathbb{G}^q_{a,\mathrm{S}}) = \coprod_{m \in \mathrm{M}} \mathrm{C}_m. \] We see at once that each \(\mathrm{C}_m\) is free of finite type over A. (Indeed, we have \(\mathrm{C}_m = 0\) if \(m\) is not a multiple of \(\alpha\); if \(m = k\alpha\), \(\mathrm{C}_m\) is isomorphic to the A-module of homogeneous polynomials of degree \(k\) in \(q\) letters). Set \[ \widehat{\mathrm{B}}_m = \varprojlim_n \mathrm{B}_m \otimes_\mathrm{A} (\mathrm{A}/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}), \qquad \widehat{\mathrm{C}}_m = \varprojlim_n \mathrm{C}_m \otimes_\mathrm{A} (\mathrm{A}/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}), \] \[ \widehat{\mathrm{B}} = \coprod_{m \in \mathrm{M}} \widehat{\mathrm{B}}_m, \qquad \widehat{\mathrm{C}} = \coprod_{m \in \mathrm{M}} \widehat{\mathrm{C}}_m. \] We then have canonical morphisms of graded algebras of type M \[ g_\mathrm{B} : \mathrm{B} \longrightarrow \widehat{\mathrm{B}} \qquad \text{and} \qquad g_\mathrm{C} : \mathrm{C} \longrightarrow \widehat{\mathrm{C}}. \] It follows from the remark made above that \(g_\mathrm{C}\) is an isomorphism. Giving a coherent system \((f_n)\) as in the statement is equivalent to giving a morphism of graded A-algebras \[ \widehat{\mathrm{F}} : \widehat{\mathrm{B}} \longrightarrow \widehat{\mathrm{C}}. \] Finding a morphism \(f\) as in the statement is equivalent to finding a morphism of graded A-algebras \(\mathrm{F} : \mathrm{B} \to \mathrm{C}\) making the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{B} \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{F}} \ar[d]_-{g_\mathrm{B}} & \mathrm{C} \ar[d]^-{g_\mathrm{C}} \\ \widehat{\mathrm{B}} \ar[r]_-{\widehat{\mathrm{F}}} & \widehat{\mathrm{C}}. } \] commute. Since \(g_\mathrm{C}\) is an isomorphism, the existence and uniqueness of F are immediate.
To complete the proof of 1.5, it therefore only remains to prove 1.9.

Proof of 1.9. We have \(\mathfrak{g} = \mathfrak{t} \oplus \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \oplus \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\). Consider \(\mathfrak{g}\) as a \(\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,k}\)-module as explained in 1.9. It is clear that \(\mathfrak{t} \oplus \mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) is a submodule of \(\mathfrak{g}\), the quotient being isomorphic to \(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\) as a \(k\)-vector space and even as a T-module. It is clear that \(\mathbb{G}_{a,k}\) cannot act nontrivially on this quotient, which is of dimension 1 (for a group morphism from \(\mathbb{G}_{a,k}\) to \(\mathbb{G}_{m,k}\) is trivial). Likewise \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) is a submodule of \(\mathfrak{t} \oplus \mathfrak{g}^\alpha\), the quotient being, as a T-module, isomorphic to \(\mathfrak{t}\), with \(\mathbb{G}_{a,k}\) acting trivially on it. In summary:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.13

Under the conditions of 1.9, \(\mathfrak{g}\) admits a composition series as a \((\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,k})\)-module whose successive quotients are \(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\), \(\mathfrak{t}\), \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\), considered as \((\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,k})\)-modules via the projection \(\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,k} \to \mathrm{T}\).

We are thus reduced to computing the cohomology of \(\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,k}\) acting via the projection \(\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,k} \to \mathrm{T}\) and the character \(\beta\) of T (here \(\beta = 0\), \(\alpha\) or \(-\alpha\)) on \(\mathrm{W}(k)\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.14

With the preceding notation, we have \[ \mathrm{H}^n(\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a,k}, k) = \mathrm{H}^n(\mathrm{C}^*_{\alpha, \beta}), \] where the complex \(\mathrm{C}^*_{\alpha, \beta}\) is defined by \[ \mathrm{C}^n_{\alpha, \beta} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \beta \neq q\alpha, \\ \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \text{homogeneous polynomials of degree } q, \text{ in } n \text{ variables} \\ \text{with coefficients in } k \end{array} \right\} & \text{if } \beta = q\alpha, \quad q \text{ an integer} > 0, \end{cases} \] \[ \delta f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{n+1}) = f(x_2, \ldots, x_{n+1}) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} (-1)^i f(x_1, \ldots, x_i x_{i+1}, \ldots, x_{n+1}) + (-1)^{n+1} f(x_1, \ldots, x_n). \]

Indeed, the functor \(\mathrm{M} \mapsto \mathrm{H}^0(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{M})\) is exact on the category of T-\(k\)-modules (and the \(\mathrm{H}^q(\mathrm{T}, -)\) vanish), by Exp. I 5.3.2. It follows, as in the usual case of group cohomology, that \(\mathrm{H}^n(\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbb{G}_{a\,k}, k)\) can be computed as the \(n\)-th cohomology group of the complex of cochains of \(\mathbb{G}_{a,k}\) in \(k\) that are invariant under T, that is, satisfying \[ f\big(\alpha(t)x_1, \ldots, \alpha(t)x_n\big) = \beta(t) f(x_1, \ldots, x_n). \] This indeed gives the announced complex.
To prove 1.9, it therefore suffices to prove that \(\mathrm{H}^2(\mathrm{C}^*_{\alpha, \beta}) = 0\) for \(\beta = 0, \alpha, -\alpha\), which is done immediately.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.15

One can compute the groups \(\mathrm{H}^n(\mathrm{C}^*_{\alpha, \beta})\) explicitly for \(\beta = q\alpha\) (see LAZARD, Lois de groupes et analyseurs, Annales E.N.S., 1955). In particular, one finds \(\mathrm{H}^n(\mathrm{C}^*_{\alpha, q\alpha}) = 0\) for \(n > q\).

blocks.title: Notations
blocks.html:
Notations 1.16

The image of the canonical immersion \[ \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}) \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{T} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha) \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] will be denoted \(\Omega\). It is an open subscheme of G containing the unit section. The image of \[ \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}), \quad \text{resp. } \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha), \quad \text{resp. } \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}) \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{T}, \quad \text{resp. } \mathrm{T} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha) \] will be denoted \[ \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}, \quad \text{resp. } \mathrm{U}_\alpha, \quad \text{resp. } \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha} \cdot \mathrm{T}, \quad \text{resp. } \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{U}_\alpha. \] Then \(\mathrm{U}_\alpha\) (resp. \(\mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}\)) is a subgroup of G canonically endowed with a vector bundle structure and we have \[ \mathrm{int}(t)(x) = x^{\alpha(t)} \qquad (\text{resp. } x^{\alpha(t)^{-1}}), \] for all \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\), \(t \in \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(x \in \mathrm{U}_\alpha(\mathrm{S}')\) (resp. \(x \in \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}(\mathrm{S}')\)). We have canonical isomorphisms \[ \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{U}_\alpha \simeq \mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathrm{U}_\alpha \qquad \text{and} \qquad \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha} \simeq \mathrm{T} \cdot_{-\alpha} \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}. \] The open subscheme \(\Omega\) is stable under \(\mathrm{int}(\mathrm{T})\): we have \[ \mathrm{int}(t')(y \cdot t \cdot x) = y^{\alpha(t')^{-1}} \cdot t \cdot x^{\alpha(t')}. \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.17

We have \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{U}_\alpha/\mathrm{S}) = \mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}/\mathrm{S}) = \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\). The isomorphisms \[ \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha) \xrightarrow{\ \exp\ } \mathrm{U}_\alpha, \qquad \text{resp. } \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}) \xrightarrow{\ \exp\ } \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}, \] are those of Exp. XIX 4.2.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.18

The open subscheme \(\Omega\) is relatively schematically dense in G (cf. XVIII, § 1).
Clear by Exp. XVIII, 1.3.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.19

The center of G is \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(\mathrm{G}) = \mathrm{Ker}(\alpha)\). It is therefore a closed subgroup of G of multiplicative type and of finite type.

The second assertion follows from the first by Exp. IX 2.7. Let us therefore prove the first. The inner automorphism defined by a section of \(\mathrm{Ker}(\alpha)\) acts trivially on \(\Omega\) (last formula of 1.16), hence on G by 1.18. Conversely, if \(g \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\) centralizes G, then it centralizes T (resp. \(\mathrm{U}_\alpha\)), hence is a section of T (Exp. XIX 2.8) (resp. which annihilates \(\alpha\)); since this also holds after any base change, we indeed have \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(\mathrm{G}) = \mathrm{Ker}(\alpha)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.20

For there to exist a monomorphism \(p_\alpha : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{G}\) normalized by T with multiplier \(\alpha\), it is necessary and sufficient that the \(\mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) be free. More precisely, we have a one-to-one correspondence (via \(p_\alpha \mapsto \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(p_\alpha)\) and \(\mathrm{X}_\alpha \mapsto (x \mapsto \exp(x\mathrm{X}_\alpha))\)) between \(\Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^*\) and the set of monomorphisms \(p_\alpha\) as above (which is also the set of isomorphisms of vector group schemes \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{U}_\alpha\)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.21

The subgroups \(\mathrm{U}_\alpha\) and \(\mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}\) of G do not commute on any fiber.
If \((\mathrm{U}_\alpha)_s\) and \((\mathrm{U}_{-\alpha})_s\) commute, then \(\Omega_s\) is a subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\), hence \(\Omega_s = \mathrm{G}_s\) and \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is solvable.
2. Structure of elementary systems

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1

Let S be a prescheme and \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) an S-elementary system. There exist a morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules \[ \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S}} \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha} \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S}, \qquad (\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}) \longmapsto \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y} \rangle, \] and a morphism of S-groups \[ \alpha^* : \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{T} \] such that for every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\), and all \(\mathrm{X} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}', \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \otimes \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{S}'})\), \(\mathrm{Y} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}', \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha} \otimes \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{S}'})\) we have: \[ \exp(\mathrm{X}) \cdot \exp(\mathrm{Y}) \in \Omega(\mathrm{S}') \iff 1 + \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y} \rangle \in \mathbb{G}_m(\mathrm{S}'), \] and under these conditions the formula: \[ (\mathrm{F}) \qquad \boxed{\ \exp(\mathrm{X}) \cdot \exp(\mathrm{Y}) = \exp\!\left( \frac{\mathrm{Y}}{1 + \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y} \rangle} \right) \alpha^*(1 + \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y} \rangle) \exp\!\left( \frac{\mathrm{X}}{1 + \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y} \rangle} \right).\ } \] Moreover, the morphisms \((\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}) \mapsto \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y} \rangle\) and \(\alpha^*\) are uniquely determined; the first is an isomorphism, hence puts the Modules \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) and \(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\) in duality, and we have \(\alpha \circ \alpha^* = 2\) (squaring in \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\)).

In view of the uniqueness assertions of the theorem, it suffices to carry out the proof locally on S. We may therefore assume \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) and \(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\) free over S. Then take \(\mathrm{X} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^*\), \(\mathrm{Y} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})^*\) and set \(p_\alpha(x) = \exp(x\mathrm{X})\), \(p_{-\alpha}(y) = \exp(y\mathrm{Y})\), for \(x, y \in \mathbb{G}_a(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\). By 1.5 and 1.21, it suffices to prove:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group, T a torus of G, \(\alpha\) a character of T nontrivial on each fiber, and \(p_\alpha : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{G}\) (resp. \(p_{-\alpha} : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{G}\)) a group monomorphism normalized by T with multiplier \(\alpha\). Suppose that:

(i) The morphism \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{T} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{G}\) defined by \((y, t, x) \mapsto p_{-\alpha}(y)\, t\, p_\alpha(x)\) is an open immersion. Its image is denoted \(\Omega\).

(ii) For every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(p_{\alpha,s}(\mathbb{G}_{a,k(s)})\) and \(p_{-\alpha,s}(\mathbb{G}_{a,k(s)})\) do not commute.

There exist an \(a \in \mathbb{G}_a(\mathrm{S})\) and an \(\alpha^* \in \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\text{S-gr.}}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}, \mathrm{T})\), uniquely determined, with the following properties: for every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and all \(x, y \in \mathbb{G}_a(\mathrm{S}')\), we have \(p_\alpha(x) p_{-\alpha}(y) \in \Omega(\mathrm{S}')\) if and only if \(1 + axy \in \mathbb{G}_m(\mathrm{S}')\), and, under these conditions, we have the formula \[ p_\alpha(x)\, p_{-\alpha}(y) = p_{-\alpha}\!\left( \frac{y}{1 + axy} \right) \alpha^*(1 + axy)\, p_\alpha\!\left( \frac{x}{1 + axy} \right). \] Moreover, \(a\) is invertible (i.e. \(a \in \mathbb{G}_m(\mathrm{S})\)) and \(\alpha \circ \alpha^* = 2\).
Proof:

A) Consider the morphism \[ \mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] defined by \((x, y) \mapsto p_\alpha(x)\, p_{-\alpha}(y)\). Let U be the inverse image of \(\Omega\) under this morphism. It is an open subscheme of \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}}\), containing \(0 \times_\mathrm{S} \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) and \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \times_\mathrm{S} 0\). There exist morphisms \[ \mathrm{A} : \mathrm{U} \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}, \qquad \mathrm{C} : \mathrm{U} \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}, \qquad \mathrm{B} : \mathrm{U} \longrightarrow \mathrm{T} \] satisfying the set-theoretic relation: \[ p_\alpha(u)\, p_{-\alpha}(v) = p_{-\alpha}(\mathrm{A}(u, v))\, \mathrm{B}(u, v)\, p_\alpha(\mathrm{C}(u, v)). \] We immediately have the relations \[ \mathrm{A}(0, v) = v, \quad \mathrm{A}(u, 0) = 0, \quad \mathrm{B}(u, 0) = \mathrm{B}(0, v) = e, \quad \mathrm{C}(u, 0) = u, \quad \mathrm{C}(0, v) = 0. \] Let \(\mathrm{S}'\) be an S-scheme and let \(t \in \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S}')\) be a point of T. Since \(\Omega_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is stable under \(\mathrm{int}(t)\), \(\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is stable under the automorphism \((x, y) \mapsto (\alpha(t)x, \alpha(t)^{-1}y)\) of \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}'}\), and we have the relations: \[ \mathrm{A}\big(\alpha(t)u, \alpha(t)^{-1}v\big) = \alpha(t)^{-1}\mathrm{A}(u, v), \quad \mathrm{C}\big(\alpha(t)u, \alpha(t)^{-1}v\big) = \alpha(t)\mathrm{C}(u, v), \quad \mathrm{B}\big(\alpha(t)u, \alpha(t)^{-1}v\big) = \mathrm{B}(u, v). \] Since \(\alpha\) is faithfully flat, we deduce that for every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and every \(z \in \mathbb{G}_m(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is stable under the transformation \((x, y) \mapsto (zx, z^{-1}y)\) and that we have \[ \mathrm{A}(zu, z^{-1}v) = z^{-1}\mathrm{A}(u, v), \quad \mathrm{C}(zu, z^{-1}v) = z\mathrm{C}(u, v), \quad \mathrm{B}(zu, z^{-1}v) = \mathrm{B}(u, v). \] Suppose first that \(v\) is invertible; taking \(z = v\), we deduce that if \((u, v)\) is a section of U, then \((uv, 1)\) is one as well and that we have \[ \mathrm{A}(uv, 1) = v^{-1}\mathrm{A}(u, v), \qquad \mathrm{B}(uv, 1) = \mathrm{B}(u, v). \] Let then V be the open subscheme of \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) defined by \[ (u, v) \in \mathrm{V}(\mathrm{S}') \iff (u, v) \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}') \text{ and } (uv, 1) \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}'). \] It is a neighborhood of the zero section of \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) and we have just seen that the morphisms \[ (u, v) \mapsto \mathrm{A}(u, v) \quad \text{and} \quad (u, v) \mapsto v\mathrm{A}(uv, 1) \] resp. \[ (u, v) \mapsto \mathrm{B}(u, v) \quad \text{and} \quad (u, v) \mapsto \mathrm{B}(uv, 1) \] coincide in \(\mathrm{V} \cap (\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}})\). Since \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\) is schematically dense in \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}}\), these morphisms coincide in V. We know that \(\mathrm{A}(0, 1) = 1\); it follows that there exists an open subscheme W of \(\mathbb{G}_{a\,\mathrm{S}}\) containing the zero section, such that for every section \(x\) of W, \(\mathrm{A}(x, 1)\) is invertible; setting \(\mathrm{A}(x, 1)^{-1} = \mathrm{F}(x)\) and arguing likewise for C, we deduce the following result:

There exist an open subscheme W of \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) containing the zero section, and S-morphisms \[ \mathrm{F} : \mathrm{W} \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}, \quad \mathrm{F}(0) = 1, \qquad \mathrm{H} : \mathrm{W} \longrightarrow \mathrm{T}, \quad \mathrm{H}(0) = e, \qquad \mathrm{G} : \mathrm{W} \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}, \quad \mathrm{G}(0) = 1, \] such that if \((u, v) \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}')\) and \(uv \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\), we have \[ (+) \qquad p_\alpha(u)\, p_{-\alpha}(v) = p_{-\alpha}(v\mathrm{F}(uv)^{-1})\, \mathrm{H}(uv)\, p_\alpha(u\mathrm{G}(uv)^{-1}). \]

B) Let us now use the associativity of G to write \[ p_\alpha(u)\, p_{-\alpha}(v)\, p_{-\alpha}(w) = p_\alpha(u)\, p_{-\alpha}(v + w). \] There exists an open subscheme L of \(\mathbb{G}^3_{a,\mathrm{S}}\), containing the unit section, such that \((u, v, w) \in \mathrm{L}(\mathrm{S}')\) is equivalent to \[ (u, v) \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}'), \quad (u\mathrm{G}(uv)^{-1}, w) \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}'), \quad (u, v + w) \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}'), \] \[ uv \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}'), \quad uw\mathrm{G}(uv)^{-1} \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}'), \quad u(v + w) \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}'). \] Then using formula (+), one immediately writes, for \((u, v, w) \in \mathrm{L}(\mathrm{S}')\), the relations: \[ \begin{aligned} (1) \quad & \mathrm{G}(uv + uw) = \mathrm{G}(uw\mathrm{G}(uv)^{-1})\mathrm{G}(uv), \\ (2) \quad & \mathrm{H}(uv + uw) = \mathrm{H}(uw\mathrm{G}(uv)^{-1})\mathrm{H}(uv), \\ (3) \quad & (v + w)\mathrm{F}(uv + uw)^{-1} = \alpha(\mathrm{H}(uv)^{-1})\, w\, \mathrm{F}(uw\mathrm{G}(uv)^{-1})^{-1} + v\mathrm{F}(uv)^{-1}. \end{aligned} \] It is immediate from the definition of L that \((1, 0, 0) \in \mathrm{L}(\mathrm{S})\). So consider \(\mathrm{L} \cap 1 \times_\mathrm{S} \mathbb{G}_{a\,\mathrm{S}} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathbb{G}_{a\,\mathrm{S}} = 1 \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{M}\); M is an open subscheme of \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}}\), containing the section \((0, 0)\), and for \((x, y) \in \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}')\), we have \[ \begin{aligned} (1') \quad & \mathrm{G}(x + y) = \mathrm{G}(y\mathrm{G}(x)^{-1})\, \mathrm{G}(x), \\ (2') \quad & \mathrm{H}(x + y) = \mathrm{H}(y\mathrm{G}(x)^{-1})\, \mathrm{H}(x), \\ (3') \quad & (x + y)\, \mathrm{F}(x + y)^{-1} = \alpha(\mathrm{H}(x))^{-1}\, y\mathrm{F}(y\mathrm{G}(x)^{-1})^{-1} + x\mathrm{F}(x)^{-1}. \end{aligned} \] Finally, consider the morphism from M to \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) defined set-theoretically by \((x, y) \mapsto (x, y\mathrm{G}(x)^{-1})\). It preserves the section \((0, 0)\). The inverse image N of M under this morphism is therefore an open subscheme of \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) containing the zero section, and we have proved the following assertion:

There exists an open subscheme N of \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}}\), containing the zero section, such that if \((x, y) \in \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{S}')\), then \(x \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(y \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(y\mathrm{G}(x) \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\) and we have the relations: \[ \begin{aligned} (1'') \quad & \mathrm{G}(x + y\mathrm{G}(x)) = \mathrm{G}(x)\mathrm{G}(y), \\ (2'') \quad & \mathrm{H}(x + y\mathrm{G}(x)) = \mathrm{H}(x)\mathrm{H}(y), \\ (3'') \quad & (x + y\mathrm{G}(x))\, \mathrm{F}(x + y\mathrm{G}(x))^{-1} = x\mathrm{F}(x)^{-1} + \alpha(\mathrm{H}(x))^{-1}\, y\mathrm{G}(x)\mathrm{F}(y)^{-1}. \end{aligned} \]
C) Arguing likewise with associativity on the left, one proves the following assertion:

There exists an open subscheme N′ of \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}}\), containing the zero section, such that if \((x, y) \in \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{S}')\), then \(x \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(y \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(y\mathrm{F}(x) \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\) and we have \[ \begin{aligned} (4'') \quad & \mathrm{F}(x + y\mathrm{F}(x)) = \mathrm{F}(x)\, \mathrm{F}(y), \\ (5'') \quad & \mathrm{H}(x + y\mathrm{F}(x)) = \mathrm{H}(x)\, \mathrm{H}(y), \\ (6'') \quad & (x + y\mathrm{F}(x))\, \mathrm{G}(x + y\mathrm{F}(x))^{-1} = x\mathrm{G}(x)^{-1} + \alpha(\mathrm{H}(x))^{-1}\, y\mathrm{F}(x)\mathrm{G}(y)^{-1}. \end{aligned} \] We are thus led to solve the "functional equation" (1″).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.3

Let S be a prescheme, W an open subscheme of \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) containing the unit section, and \(\mathrm{F} : \mathrm{W} \to \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\) an S-morphism. Suppose that \(\mathrm{F}(0) = 1\) and that there exists an open subscheme N of \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) containing the zero section such that for \((x, y) \in \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{S}')\), we have \(x \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(y \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(y\mathrm{F}(x) \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\) and \(\mathrm{F}(x + y\mathrm{F}(x)) = \mathrm{F}(x)\mathrm{F}(y)\).

(i) If S is the spectrum of a field \(k\), there exists an \(a \in k\) with \(\mathrm{F}(x) = 1 + ax\).

(ii) If \(a = \mathrm{F}'(0) \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S})\) is invertible, then \(\mathrm{F}(x) = 1 + ax\).

By virtue of the hypotheses, we may differentiate the given equation at \(x = 0\) (resp. at \(y = 0\)) and we find that \[ (*) \qquad \mathrm{F}'(y)\,(1 + y\mathrm{F}'(0)) = \mathrm{F}'(0)\, \mathrm{F}(y) \qquad \text{for } (0, y) \in \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{S}'), \] resp. \[ \mathrm{F}'(x)\, \mathrm{F}(x) = \mathrm{F}(x)\, \mathrm{F}'(0) \qquad \text{for } (x, 0) \in \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{S}'). \] Since F takes its values in \(\mathbb{G}_m\), the second relation gives us \[ (*') \qquad \mathrm{F}'(x) = \mathrm{F}'(0) \qquad \text{for } (x, 0) \in \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{S}')\,; \] whence, by the first, \[ \mathrm{F}'(0)(1 + y\mathrm{F}'(0)) = \mathrm{F}'(0)\mathrm{F}(y) \qquad \text{for } (y, 0) \in \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{S}'), (0, y) \in \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{S}'). \] If \(a = \mathrm{F}'(0)\) is invertible, this gives us \[ \mathrm{F}(y) = 1 + ay, \] for \(y\) a section of an open subscheme of W containing the unit section, hence schematically dense in W, which proves (ii). This also proves (i) when \(\mathrm{F}'(0) \neq 0\).

If \(\mathrm{F}'(0) = 0\), then \(\mathrm{F}'(x) = 0\) when \(x\) is "close to 0", hence \(\mathrm{F}' = 0\) by schematic density. If \(k\) is of characteristic 0, F is a rational function with vanishing derivative, hence constant. If \(k\) is of characteristic \(p\), and if F is not constant, consider F as a formal power series with positive coefficients and write \[ \mathrm{F}(x) = \mathrm{F}_1(x^{p^n}), \quad \mathrm{F}'_1(x) \neq 0, \quad n > 0, \quad \mathrm{F}_1 \in k[[x]]. \] Substituting into the functional equation, we find \[ \mathrm{F}_1(x + y\mathrm{F}_1(x)^{p^n}) = \mathrm{F}_1(x)\mathrm{F}_1(y)\,; \] differentiating at \(x = 0\), we obtain \[ \mathrm{F}'_1(x)\mathrm{F}_1(x)^{p^n - 1} = \mathrm{F}'_1(0), \] which is absurd.

D) Suppose S is the spectrum of a field. If \(\mathrm{F}'(0) = 0\), then \(\mathrm{F} = 1\). Formula (5″) then gives us \(\mathrm{H}(x + y) = \mathrm{H}(x)\mathrm{H}(y)\), which shows that H extends to a group morphism \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{T}\) (Exp. XVIII 2.3), which is necessarily constant with value \(e\). We will also have \(\mathrm{G}(x) = 1 + bx\), but formula (6″) then gives \(b = 0\), which shows that F, G, H are constant with value \((0, e, 0)\) in a neighborhood of the unit section, hence everywhere, which by (+) shows that \(\mathrm{U}_\alpha\) and \(\mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}\) commute, contrary to hypothesis (ii).

If S is now arbitrary, we have therefore proved that \(\mathrm{F}'(0)\) does not vanish on any fiber, hence is invertible. The same evidently holds for \(\mathrm{G}'(0)\), which, by Lemma 2.3, shows that there exist \(a, b \in \mathbb{G}_m(\mathrm{S})\) with \[ (\diamondsuit_1) \qquad \mathrm{F}(x) = 1 + ax, \quad \mathrm{G}(x) = 1 + bx, \qquad \text{for } x \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}'). \]

E) The rest is now easy. Substituting the preceding results into (3″), we immediately find \[ y\, \alpha(\mathrm{H}(x)) = y\Big(1 + ax + ay(1 + bx)\Big)(1 + bx)(1 + ay)^{-1}. \] This formula is valid for a section \((x, y)\) of N. But since \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\) is schematically dense in \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}}\), we deduce \[ \alpha(\mathrm{H}(x)) = \Big(1 + ax + ay(1 + bx)\Big)(1 + bx)(1 + ay)^{-1}. \] Setting \(y = 0\), this gives \(\alpha(\mathrm{H}(x)) = (1 + ax)(1 + bx)\). Identifying the two preceding expressions, we find \(a^2 xy = abxy\). Arguing as before, we deduce \(a^2 = ab\), and since \(a\) is invertible, \(a = b\), whence \(\alpha(\mathrm{H}(x)) = (1 + ax)^2\). Since \(a\) is invertible, \(x \mapsto 1 + ax\) is an automorphism of \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\). We can therefore find an open subscheme W′ of \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) containing the section 1 and a morphism \[ \mathrm{P} : \mathrm{W}' \longrightarrow \mathrm{T} \] such that \(\mathrm{P}(1 + ax) = \mathrm{H}(x)\). Substituting into relation (2″), we immediately find, for \((x, y) \in \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{S}')\), \[ \mathrm{P}(1 + ax + ay) = \mathrm{P}\!\left( \frac{1 + ax + ay}{1 + ax} \right) \mathrm{P}(1 + ax), \] which proves that there exists an open neighborhood of 1 in \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\) such that for \(x\) and \(y\) in this neighborhood we have \(\mathrm{P}(x)\mathrm{P}(y) = \mathrm{P}(xy)\). By virtue of Exp. XVIII 2.3, there exists a group morphism \[ \alpha^* : \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{T} \] which extends P. Since \(\alpha(\mathrm{H}(x)) = (1 + ax)^2\) in a neighborhood of the section 0, we have \(\alpha(\alpha^*(z)) = z^2\) in a neighborhood of the section 1, hence \[ \alpha \circ \alpha^* = 2. \]

F) Gathering the results obtained, we see that there exist an open subscheme V′ of \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) containing the unit section, an \(a \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\), and an \(\alpha^* \in \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\text{S-gr}}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}, \mathrm{T})\), such that \(\alpha \circ \alpha^* = 2\), and such that for \((u, v) \in \mathrm{V}'(\mathrm{S}')\), \(1 + auv\) is invertible and we have \[ p_\alpha(u)p_{-\alpha}(v) = p_{-\alpha}\!\left( \frac{v}{1 + auv} \right) \alpha^*(1 + auv)\, p_\alpha\!\left( \frac{u}{1 + auv} \right). \] Consider the open subscheme V of \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) defined by "\(1 + auv\) invertible", i.e. \(\mathrm{V} = (\mathbb{G}^2_{a,\mathrm{S}})_f\) where \(f(u, v) = 1 + auv\). The two sides of the preceding formula define morphisms from V to G which coincide in a neighborhood of the section 0, hence coincide in V. The preceding formula is therefore valid for every section \((u, v)\) of V. It follows in particular that \(\mathrm{V} \subset \mathrm{U}\), where U is the open subscheme of A). Let us prove that \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{V}\). Returning to the notation of A), we have a morphism \(\mathrm{A} : \mathrm{U} \to \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) which, on V, is defined by \(\mathrm{A}(u, v) = v(1 + auv)^{-1}\). To show that \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{V}\), which is a set-theoretic question, we are reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of a field \(k\), hence to the following obvious assertion: the domain of definition of the rational map \(\mathbb{G}^2_{a,k} \to \mathbb{G}_{a\,k}\) defined by the rational function \(\dfrac{\mathrm{Y}}{1 + a\mathrm{XY}}\) is the open set defined by the function \(1 + a\mathrm{XY}\).

G) We have thus proved the existence of \(a\) and of \(\alpha^*\), as well as the two additional properties announced. It remains to prove uniqueness. So let \(a'\) and \(\alpha^{*\prime}\) also satisfy the required conditions. If \(u, v \in \mathbb{G}_a(\mathrm{S}')^2\), we have at once: \[ 1 + auv \text{ invertible} \Rightarrow 1 + a'uv \text{ invertible and } \frac{v}{1 + auv} = \frac{v}{1 + a'uv}\,; \] we therefore have, for every section \(u\) of \(\mathbb{G}_a(\mathrm{S}')\), \[ 1 + au \text{ invertible} \implies 1 + au = 1 + a'u, \] which immediately proves \(a = a'\). With the same notation, we then have \[ 1 + au \text{ invertible} \implies \alpha^*(1 + au) = \alpha^{*\prime}(1 + au), \] hence also \(\alpha^* = \alpha^{*\prime}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4

Let \(\exp(\mathrm{Y})\, t\, \exp(\mathrm{X})\) and \(\exp(\mathrm{Y}')\, t'\, \exp(\mathrm{X}')\) be two elements of \(\Omega(\mathrm{S}')\). Then their product is in \(\Omega(\mathrm{S}')\) if and only if \(u = 1 + \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}' \rangle\) is invertible, and we have \[ (\mathrm{F}') \qquad \begin{aligned} &\exp(\mathrm{Y})\, t\, \exp(\mathrm{X}) \cdot \exp(\mathrm{Y}')\, t'\, \exp(\mathrm{X}') = \\ &\qquad \exp(\mathrm{Y} + u^{-1}\alpha(t)^{-1}\mathrm{Y}') \cdot tt'\alpha^*(u) \cdot \exp(u^{-1}\alpha(t')^{-1}\mathrm{X} + \mathrm{X}'). \end{aligned} \]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.5

Formula (F) can also be written without involving the exp morphisms. Transporting the duality \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha \otimes \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha} \to \mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S}\) by these morphisms, we obtain a canonical pairing of vector bundles: \[ \mathrm{U}_\alpha \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha} \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}, \] which we will still denote \((x, y) \mapsto \langle x, y \rangle\). We thus have \(\langle \exp \mathrm{X}, \exp \mathrm{Y} \rangle = \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y} \rangle\). If \(x \in \mathrm{U}_\alpha(\mathrm{S}')\), \(y \in \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}(\mathrm{S}')\) and if \(1 + \langle x, y \rangle \in \mathbb{G}_m(\mathrm{S}')\), we have \[ (\mathrm{F}) \qquad x \cdot y = y^{(1 + \langle x, y \rangle)^{-1}} \cdot \alpha^*(1 + \langle x, y \rangle) \cdot x^{(1 + \langle x, y \rangle)^{-1}}. \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.6

The pairing \[ \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha) \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}) \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \] defines a pairing of principal bundles under \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\) \[ \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^* \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})^* \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}. \]
This pairing will be denoted \((\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}) \mapsto \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y} \rangle\), or more simply \((\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}) \mapsto \mathrm{XY}\).

For every section \(\mathrm{X} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^*\), there thus exists a unique section \(\mathrm{X}^{-1}\) of \(\Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})^*\) such that \(\mathrm{X}\mathrm{X}^{-1} = 1\). We have \((z\mathrm{X})^{-1} = z^{-1}\mathrm{X}^{-1}\). The morphism \[ s : \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^* \longrightarrow \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})^* \] thus defined is therefore an isomorphism of preschemes, compatible with the isomorphism \(s : z \mapsto z^{-1}\) on the groups of operators.
We will often say that X and \(s(\mathrm{X}) = \mathrm{X}^{-1}\) are paired.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.7

Let \(\mathrm{X} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^*\) and \(a \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S})^*\). We have \[ \alpha^*(a) = \exp((a^{-1} - 1)\mathrm{X}^{-1})\, \exp(\mathrm{X})\, \exp((a - 1)\mathrm{X}^{-1})\, \exp(-a^{-1}\mathrm{X}). \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.8

The morphism \(\alpha^*\) is called the coroot associated with the root \(\alpha\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.9

If \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) is an S-elementary system, so is \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, -\alpha)\). By Theorem 2.1 we therefore have a duality between \(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\) and \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\), and a coroot \((-\alpha)^*\). Taking the inverse of formula (F), one immediately proves \[ \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y} \rangle = \langle \mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{X} \rangle, \qquad (-\alpha)^* = -\alpha^*. \]

Let us now pass to the Lie Algebra of G. The root \(\alpha\) and the coroot \(\alpha^*\) define the linear forms \[ \mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S} \xrightarrow{\ \overline{\alpha^*}\ } \mathfrak{t} \xrightarrow{\ \overline{\alpha}\ } \mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S}. \] We will write \(\mathrm{H}_\alpha = \overline{\alpha^*}(1)\). We call \(\overline{\alpha}\) the infinitesimal root associated with \(\alpha\), and \(\mathrm{H}_\alpha\) the corresponding infinitesimal coroot.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.10

Let \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and \(\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{X}' \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{t})(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{Y}' \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})(\mathrm{S}')\), \(t \in \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S}')\). We have \[ (1) \qquad \mathrm{Ad}(t)\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{H}, \qquad \mathrm{Ad}(t)\mathrm{X} = \alpha(t)\mathrm{X}, \qquad \mathrm{Ad}(t)\mathrm{Y} = \alpha(t)^{-1}\mathrm{Y}. \] \[ (2) \qquad \begin{aligned} &\mathrm{Ad}(\exp(\mathrm{X}))\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{H} - \overline{\alpha}(\mathrm{H})\mathrm{X}, \qquad \mathrm{Ad}(\exp(\mathrm{X}))\mathrm{X}' = \mathrm{X}', \\ &\mathrm{Ad}(\exp(\mathrm{X}))\mathrm{Y} = \mathrm{Y} + \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y} \rangle \mathrm{H}_\alpha - \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y} \rangle \mathrm{X}. \end{aligned} \] \[ (2') \qquad \begin{aligned} &\mathrm{Ad}(\exp(\mathrm{Y}))\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{H} + \overline{\alpha}(\mathrm{H})\mathrm{Y}, \qquad \mathrm{Ad}(\exp(\mathrm{Y}))\mathrm{Y}' = \mathrm{Y}', \\ &\mathrm{Ad}(\exp(\mathrm{Y}))\mathrm{X} = \mathrm{X} + \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y} \rangle \mathrm{H}_{-\alpha} - \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y} \rangle \mathrm{Y}. \end{aligned} \] \[ (3) \qquad [\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{X}] = \overline{\alpha}(\mathrm{H})\mathrm{X}, \qquad [\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{Y}] = -\overline{\alpha}(\mathrm{H})\mathrm{Y}, \qquad [\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}] = \langle \mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y} \rangle \mathrm{H}_\alpha. \] \[ (4) \qquad \mathrm{H}_{-\alpha} = -\mathrm{H}_\alpha. \] \[ (5) \qquad \overline{\alpha}(\mathrm{H}_\alpha) = 2. \]
The proof of these various formulas is either trivial or an immediate consequence of (F).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.11

Suppose \(\mathrm{H}_\alpha\) is nonzero on every fiber (which is in particular the case if 2 is invertible on S, by (5)). Then \(\mathrm{X}_\alpha \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^*\) and \(\mathrm{X}_{-\alpha} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})^*\) are paired if and only if \([\mathrm{X}_\alpha, \mathrm{X}_{-\alpha}] = \mathrm{H}_\alpha\).

Let \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) be an S-elementary system. We know (1.19) that the center of G is \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(\mathrm{G}) = \mathrm{Ker}(\alpha)\), a group of multiplicative type and of finite type. If Q is a subgroup of multiplicative type of \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(\mathrm{G})\), the quotient \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Q}\) is affine over S (Exp. IX 2.5), smooth over S (Exp. VI) with connected and reductive fibers of semisimple rank 1 (Exp. XIX 1.8).

Set \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Q}\); it is a reductive S-group of semisimple rank 1, and \(\mathrm{T}' = \mathrm{T}/\mathrm{Q}\) is a maximal torus of it. The open subscheme \(\mathrm{U}_{-\alpha} \cdot \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{U}_\alpha\) of G is stable under Q and one sees at once that the quotient is isomorphic to \(\mathrm{U}_{-\alpha} \times_\mathrm{S} (\mathrm{T}/\mathrm{Q}) \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{U}_\alpha\). If we denote by \(\alpha'\) the character of \(\mathrm{T}'\) induced by \(\alpha\), it follows that the morphism derived from the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}'\) induces an isomorphism \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha \to \mathfrak{g}'^{\alpha'}\) (resp. \(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha} \to \mathfrak{g}'^{-\alpha'}\)). In particular, \(\alpha'\) is a root of \(\mathrm{G}'\) with respect to \(\mathrm{T}'\). Hence:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.13

If Q is a subgroup of multiplicative type of \(\mathrm{Ker}(\alpha)\), \[ (\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{T}/\mathrm{Q}, \alpha/\mathrm{Q}) \] is an elementary system.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.14

Under the preceding conditions, the following diagrams are commutative \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha) \ar[r]^-{\exp} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{can}}^-{\wr} & \mathrm{G} \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{can}} & \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}) \ar[l]_-{\exp} \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{can}}_-{\wr} \\ \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}'^{\alpha'}) \ar[r]_-{\exp} & \mathrm{G}' & \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}'^{-\alpha'}) \ar[l]^-{\exp} } \] \[ \xymatrix{ \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \otimes \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha} \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{can}}^-{\wr} & \mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S} \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{id}} \\ \mathfrak{g}'^{\alpha'} \otimes \mathfrak{g}'^{-\alpha'} \ar[r]_-{\sim} & \mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S} } \] \[ \xymatrix{ & \mathrm{T} \ar[dd]^(0.3){\mathrm{can}} \ar[dr]^-{\alpha} & \\ \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \ar[ur]^-{\alpha^*} \ar[dr]_-{\alpha'^*} & & \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \\ & \mathrm{T}' \ar[ur]_-{\alpha'} & } \]
3. The Weyl group

If \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) is an S-elementary system, we will write \[ \mathrm{N} = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T}), \qquad \mathrm{W} = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})/\mathrm{T}, \] (cf. Exp. XIX 6.3); N is a closed subgroup of G, smooth over S. We will denote by \(\mathrm{Q} = \mathrm{N} - \mathrm{T}\) the open subprescheme of N induced on the complement of T. Let R denote the (unique) maximal torus of \(\mathrm{Ker}(\alpha)\), and \(\mathrm{T}'\) the image of \(\alpha^* : \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{T}\), which is a subtorus of dimension 1 of T. The morphism \[ \mathrm{T}' \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{R} \longrightarrow \mathrm{T} \] induced by the product in T is surjective (hence faithfully flat); indeed, we are reduced to checking this on geometric fibers, and it follows at once from the formula \(\alpha \circ \alpha^* = 2\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.1

With the preceding notation:

(i) W is isomorphic to the constant group \((\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_\mathrm{S}\).

(ii) Q is a locally trivial principal homogeneous bundle under T, on the left by the law \((t, q) \mapsto tq\) (resp. on the right by the law \((q, t) \mapsto qt\)).

(iii) We have the formula \[ \mathrm{int}(w)t = t \cdot \alpha^*(\alpha(t)^{-1}) \] for \(w \in \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(t \in \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\). In the decomposition \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}'} = \mathrm{T}'_{\mathrm{S}'} \cdot \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), \(\mathrm{int}(w)\) induces the identity on \(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) and the symmetry on \(\mathrm{T}'_{\mathrm{S}'}\). We have the relations \[ \alpha \circ \mathrm{int}(w) = \alpha^{-1}, \qquad \mathrm{int}(w) \circ \alpha^* = (\alpha^*)^{-1}. \]

(iv) For \(\mathrm{X} \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^*(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\), set \[ w_\alpha(\mathrm{X}) = \exp(\mathrm{X}) \exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp(\mathrm{X}). \] Then \(w_\alpha(\mathrm{X}) \in \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{S}')\) and the morphism \(w_\alpha : \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^* \to \mathrm{Q}\) thus defined satisfies \[ w_\alpha(z\mathrm{X}) = \alpha^*(z)\, w_\alpha(\mathrm{X}) = w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})\, \alpha^*(z)^{-1}, \] for \(z \in \mathbb{G}_m(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{X} \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^*(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\).

(v) We have the relation \[ w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})\, w_\alpha(\mathrm{Y}) = w_\alpha(-\mathrm{X}\mathrm{Y}^{-1}). \] In particular, \[ w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})^2 = \alpha^*(-1) \in {}_2\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S}) \cap \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(\mathrm{G})(\mathrm{S}), \] \[ w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})^{-1} = w_\alpha(-\mathrm{X}) = \alpha^*(-1)\, w_\alpha(\mathrm{X}). \]

(vi) If we likewise define, for \(\mathrm{Y} \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})^*(\mathrm{S}')\), \[ w_{-\alpha}(\mathrm{Y}) = \exp(\mathrm{Y}) \exp(-\mathrm{Y}^{-1}) \exp(\mathrm{Y}), \] we have (in addition to the formulas analogous to the preceding ones) \[ w_{-\alpha}(\mathrm{X}^{-1}) = w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})^{-1} = w_\alpha(-\mathrm{X}), \qquad w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})\, w_{-\alpha}(\mathrm{Y}) = \alpha^*(\mathrm{XY}). \]

Proof. (i) has already been seen in Exp. XIX 2.4; it follows at once that Q is indeed a principal homogeneous bundle under T for the laws defined in (ii); the fact that it is locally trivial follows notably from (iv).

Let us prove (iii); if \(w \in \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{S})\), it is clear that \(\alpha \circ \mathrm{int}(w)\) is a root of G with respect to T, which is therefore locally equal to \(\alpha\) or \(-\alpha\); since on each fiber it is \(-\alpha\) (BIBLE, 12-05, proof of the cor. to prop. 1), we have \(\alpha \circ \mathrm{int}(w) = -\alpha\). By transport of structure, we deduce \[ -\alpha^* = \mathrm{int}(w)^{-1} \circ \alpha^* = \mathrm{int}(w) \circ \alpha^*, \] since \(\mathrm{int}(w)^2 = \mathrm{int}(w^2)\) and \(w^2\) is a section of T. Hence \(\mathrm{int}(w)\) induces the symmetry on \(\mathrm{T}'\); since R is central, \(\mathrm{int}(w)\) induces the identity on R. The formula of (iii) defines a morphism \(\mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{T}\) which satisfies the same properties, hence coincides with \(\mathrm{int}(w)\).

Let us prove (iv). We have successively \[ \begin{aligned} w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})\, t\, w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})^{-1} &= \exp(\mathrm{X}) \exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp(\mathrm{X})\, t\, \exp(-\mathrm{X}) \exp(\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp(-\mathrm{X}) \\ &= \exp(\mathrm{X}) \exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp(\mathrm{X} - \alpha(t)\mathrm{X}) \exp(\alpha(t)^{-1}\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp(-\alpha(t)\mathrm{X})\, t. \end{aligned} \] By applying formula (F), we have \[ \exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp\big((1 - \alpha(t))\mathrm{X}\big) = \exp\big((\alpha(t)^{-1} - 1)\mathrm{X}\big)\, \alpha^*(\alpha(t)^{-1}) \exp\big(-\alpha(t)^{-1}\mathrm{X}^{-1}\big). \] Substituting into the preceding relation, we find \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{int}(w_\alpha(\mathrm{X}))\, t &= \exp\big(\alpha(t)^{-1}\mathrm{X}\big)\, \alpha^*(\alpha(t)^{-1}) \exp(-\alpha(t)\mathrm{X})\, t \\ &= \exp(a\mathrm{X})\, \alpha^*(\alpha(t)^{-1})\, t, \end{aligned} \] where \[ a = \alpha(t)^{-1} - (\alpha \circ \alpha^*)(\alpha(t)^{-1})\, \alpha(t), \] but \(\alpha \circ \alpha^* = 2\), which at once gives \(a = 0\) and \(w_\alpha(\mathrm{X}) \in \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{S}')\).

Let us now prove the second assertion of (iv). We have \[ \begin{aligned} \alpha^*(z)\, w_\alpha(\mathrm{X}) &= \exp(z^2\mathrm{X}) \exp(-z^{-2}\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp(z^2\mathrm{X})\, \alpha^*(z) \\ &= \exp(z\mathrm{X}) \exp\big((z^2 - z)\mathrm{X}\big) \exp(-z^{-2}\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp(z^2\mathrm{X})\, \alpha^*(z) \\ &= \exp(z\mathrm{X}) \exp(-z^{-1}\mathrm{X}^{-1})\, \alpha^*(z)^{-1} \exp((z^3 - z^2)\mathrm{X}) \exp(z^2\mathrm{X})\, \alpha^*(z) \\ &= \exp(z\mathrm{X}) \exp(-z^{-1}\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp(z\mathrm{X}) = w_\alpha(z\mathrm{X}). \end{aligned} \]

Let us prove (v). By virtue of the preceding result, the first formula of (v) follows at once from the second; let us prove the latter: \[ \begin{aligned} w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})^2 &= \exp(\mathrm{X}) \exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp(2\mathrm{X}) \exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp(\mathrm{X}) \\ &= \exp(\mathrm{X}) \exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp(\mathrm{X}^{-1})\, \alpha^*(-1) \exp(-2\mathrm{X}) \exp(\mathrm{X}) \\ &= \exp(\mathrm{X})\, \alpha^*(-1) \exp(-\mathrm{X}) = \alpha^*(-1), \end{aligned} \] since \(\alpha(\alpha^*(-1)) = (-1)^2 = 1\), which proves that \(\alpha^*(-1) \in \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(\mathrm{G})(\mathrm{S})\).

Finally, let us prove (vi). The first assertion is a special case of the second; let us prove the latter. The two sides of this formula define morphisms from \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^* \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})^*\) to G. To prove that they coincide, it suffices to do so on an open set that is nonempty on each fiber (Exp. XVIII 1.4); it therefore suffices to check the relation when \(1 + \mathrm{XY}\) is invertible. We then have successively: \[ \begin{aligned} w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})\, w_{-\alpha}(\mathrm{Y}) &= \exp(\mathrm{X}) \exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp(\mathrm{X}) \exp(\mathrm{Y}) \exp(-\mathrm{Y}^{-1}) \exp(\mathrm{Y}) \\ &= \exp(\mathrm{X}) \exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp\!\left( \frac{\mathrm{Y}}{1 + \mathrm{XY}} \right) \alpha^*(1 + \mathrm{XY}) \exp\!\left( \frac{\mathrm{X}}{1 + \mathrm{XY}} \right) \exp(-\mathrm{Y}^{-1}) \exp(\mathrm{Y}) \\ &= \exp(\mathrm{X}) \exp\!\left( \frac{-\mathrm{X}^{-1}}{1 + \mathrm{XY}} \right) \alpha^*(1 + \mathrm{XY}) \exp\!\left( \frac{-\mathrm{Y}^{-1}}{1 + \mathrm{XY}} \right) \exp(\mathrm{Y}) \\ &= \exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-2}\mathrm{Y}^{-1})\, \alpha^*\!\left( \frac{\mathrm{XY}}{1 + \mathrm{XY}} \right) \exp(\mathrm{X} + \mathrm{Y}^{-1})\, \alpha^*(1 + \mathrm{XY}) \exp\!\left( \frac{-\mathrm{Y}^{-1}}{1 + \mathrm{XY}} \right) \exp(\mathrm{Y}) \\ &= \exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-2}\mathrm{Y}^{-1})\, \alpha^*(\mathrm{XY}) \exp\!\left( \frac{\mathrm{Y}^{-1} + \mathrm{X}}{(1 + \mathrm{XY})^2} \right) \exp\!\left( \frac{-\mathrm{Y}^{-1}}{1 + \mathrm{XY}} \right) \exp(\mathrm{Y}) \\ &= \alpha^*(\mathrm{XY}) \exp(-\mathrm{Y}) \exp(\mathrm{Y}) = \alpha^*(\mathrm{XY}). \end{aligned} \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.2

Let \(n \in \mathbb{Z}\), \(n \neq 0\). For every \(w \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\), the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(w \in \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{S})\),

(ii) we have \(\mathrm{int}(w) \circ n\alpha^* = -n\alpha^*\) (recall that \((n\alpha^*)(z) = \alpha^*(z)^n\)).
We have (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii) (assertion (iii) of the theorem); conversely, we may assume that Q has a section and we are reduced to proving:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.3

We have \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(n\alpha^*) = \mathrm{T}\) for \(n \neq 0\).

Indeed, the image \(\mathrm{T}'\) of \(n\alpha^*\) is a subtorus of G. It follows (Exp. XIX 2.8) that \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(n\alpha^*)\) is a reductive subgroup of G containing T. Since on each fiber we have \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_s}(n\alpha^*_s) \neq \mathrm{G}_s\), we have \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_s}(n\alpha^*_s) = \mathrm{T}_s\) (Exp. XIX last assertion of 1.6), hence \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(n\alpha^*) = \mathrm{T}\), since these are smooth subgroups of G.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.4

The construction of \(w_\alpha\) and the fact that \(w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})\) normalizes T rely only on formula (F). In particular, if G is an S-group satisfying the conditions of 2.2, \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})\) is different from T on each fiber. It follows that if G is an affine S-group with connected fibers satisfying the conditions of 2.2, it is reductive of semisimple rank 1. Indeed, it is smooth in a neighborhood of the unit section, hence smooth, and one can apply the criterion of Exp. XIX 1.11.

Before stating the next theorem, let us make a few remarks. As usual, we identify \(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\) with \((\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^{\otimes -1}\). Likewise, we will identify \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S}}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}, \mathfrak{g}^\alpha)\) with \((\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^{\otimes 2}\) and hence \(\underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_{\mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S}\text{-mod.}}(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}), \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha))\) with \(\mathrm{W}((\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^{\otimes 2})^*\).

If \(w \in \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{S})\), then \(\mathrm{Ad}(w)\) interchanges \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) and \(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\) (3.1, (iii)), hence defines an isomorphism: \[ a_\alpha(w) : \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathfrak{g}^\alpha, \] which we will therefore identify with a section \(a_\alpha(w) \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, (\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^{\otimes 2})^*\). This construction is compatible with base change and therefore defines a morphism \[ a_\alpha : \mathrm{Q} \longrightarrow \mathrm{W}((\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^{\otimes 2})^*. \]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.6

Let \(a_\alpha : \mathrm{Q} \to \mathrm{W}((\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^{\otimes 2})^*\) be the morphism defined by \(\mathrm{Ad}(w)\mathrm{Y} = a_\alpha(w)\mathrm{Y}\) (Y a section of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})\)).

(i) We have \[ \mathrm{int}(w) \exp(\mathrm{Y}) = \exp(a_\alpha(w)\mathrm{Y}) \] for every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and all \(w \in \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{Y} \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})(\mathrm{S}')\).

(ii) We have \[ a_\alpha(tw) = \alpha(t)\, a_\alpha(w), \qquad a_\alpha(wt) = \alpha(t)^{-1}\, a_\alpha(w). \]

(iii) If we likewise define \(a_{-\alpha} : \mathrm{Q} \to \mathrm{W}((\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})^{\otimes 2})^*\), we have \[ a_{-\alpha}(w) = a_\alpha(w)^{-1}. \]

(iv) For every \(\mathrm{X} \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^*(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\), we have \[ a_\alpha(w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})) = -\mathrm{X}^2. \]

Assertion (i) is trivial, by the characterization of the exp morphisms given in 1.5. Assertion (ii) is immediate, as is (iii). Let us prove (iv): let \(\mathrm{X} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}', \mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^*\), \(\mathrm{Z} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}', \mathfrak{g}^\alpha)\); we have by definition \[ \begin{aligned} a_\alpha(w)^{-1}\mathrm{Z} &= \mathrm{Ad}(w_\alpha(\mathrm{X}))\mathrm{Z} \\ &= \mathrm{Ad}(\exp(\mathrm{X}))\, \mathrm{Ad}(\exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-1}))\, \mathrm{Ad}(\exp(\mathrm{X}))\mathrm{Z} \\ &= \mathrm{Ad}(\exp(\mathrm{X}))\, \mathrm{Ad}(\exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-1}))\mathrm{Z} \\ &= \mathrm{Ad}(\exp(\mathrm{X}))(\mathrm{Z} - \mathrm{X}^{-1}\mathrm{Z} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{-\alpha} - \mathrm{X}^{-1}\mathrm{Z} \cdot \mathrm{X}^{-1}), \end{aligned} \] by formula (2′) of 2.10. Now applying 2.10, (2), we obtain \[ \begin{aligned} &\mathrm{Z} - \mathrm{X}^{-1}\mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{H}_{-\alpha} - 2\mathrm{X}) - \mathrm{X}^{-1}\mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{X}^{-1} + \mathrm{X}^{-1}\mathrm{X}\mathrm{H}_\alpha - \mathrm{X}\mathrm{X}^{-1}\mathrm{X}) = \\ &\qquad -\mathrm{X}^{-2}\mathrm{Z} - \mathrm{X}^{-1}\mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{H}_{-\alpha} + \mathrm{H}_\alpha) = -\mathrm{X}^{-2}\mathrm{Z}, \end{aligned} \] which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.7

In particular, we have \[ \mathrm{int}(w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})) \exp(\mathrm{X}) = \exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-1}), \] whence (by the definition of \(w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})\)): \[ w_\alpha(\mathrm{X}) \exp(\mathrm{X})\, w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})^{-1} = \exp(-\mathrm{X})\, w_\alpha(\mathrm{X}) \exp(-\mathrm{X}), \] that is, by an immediate computation, \[ \big(w_\alpha(\mathrm{X}) \exp(\mathrm{X})\big)^3 = e. \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.8

Let \(\mathrm{X} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^*\) and \(n \in \mathbb{Z}\), \(n \neq 0\). Set \(u = \exp(\mathrm{X}) \in \mathrm{U}_\alpha^*(\mathrm{S})\). Then \(w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})\) is the unique section \(w \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\) which satisfies

(i) \(\mathrm{int}(w) \circ n\alpha^* = -n\alpha^*\).

(ii) \((wu)^3 = e\).

We know that \(w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})\) does satisfy these conditions. Conversely, let \(w \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\) satisfy (i) and (ii). By 3.2 and 3.1 (ii), we know that there exists a \(t \in \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S})\) with \(w = w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})\, t\). We then have \[ w\, u\, w^{-1} = w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})\, t \exp(\mathrm{X})\, t^{-1}\, w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})^{-1} = \exp(-\alpha(t)\mathrm{X}^{-1}), \] and on the other hand \[ \begin{aligned} u^{-1}\, w\, u^{-1} &= \exp(-\mathrm{X})\, w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})\, t \exp(-\mathrm{X}) \\ &= \exp(-\mathrm{X})\, w_\alpha(\mathrm{X}) \exp(-\mathrm{X}) \exp(\mathrm{X} - \alpha(t)\mathrm{X})\, t \\ &= \exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-1}) \exp\big(\mathrm{X} - \alpha(t)\mathrm{X}\big)\, t = \exp(-\mathrm{X}^{-1})\, t \exp(\mathrm{H}). \end{aligned} \] Now \((wu)^3 = e \iff w\, u\, w^{-1} = u^{-1}\, w\, u^{-1}\); comparing the two decompositions of this element on \(\mathrm{U}_{-\alpha} \cdot \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{U}_\alpha\), we deduce \(t = e\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.9

A number of the results of this section can be summarized by the following diagram of principal homogeneous bundles (on the left) \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^* \ar[r]^-{w_\alpha} & \mathrm{Q} \ar[r]^-{a_\alpha} & \mathrm{W}((\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^{\otimes 2})^* \\ \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \ar[r]_-{\alpha^*} & \mathrm{T} \ar[r]_-{\alpha} & \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}. } \]

Note that \(a_\alpha\) is faithfully flat (\(\alpha\) being so) and that \(w_\alpha\) is a monomorphism if and only if \(\alpha^*\) is a monomorphism. We leave it to the reader to write out the corresponding diagrams for the right principal bundle structures, as well as the diagrams of the same kind for the root \(-\alpha\), and to study the relations between these various diagrams.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.10

Let S be a prescheme, \(q\) an integer \(> 0\) such that \(x \mapsto x^q\) defines an endomorphism of \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\), \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) and \((\mathrm{G}', \mathrm{T}', \alpha')\) two S-elementary systems, and \(f : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}'\) a morphism of S-groups. Let \[ h : (\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^{\otimes q} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathfrak{g}'^{\alpha'} \] be an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules and \[ h^\vee : (\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})^{\otimes q} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathfrak{g}'^{-\alpha'} \] the contragredient isomorphism. Suppose that for every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and every \(\mathrm{X} \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)(\mathrm{S}')\), we have \(f(\exp(\mathrm{X})) = \exp(h(\mathrm{X}^q))\). The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(f(\alpha^*(z)) = \alpha'^*(z)^q\).

(ii) \(f(w_\alpha(\mathrm{Z})) = w_{\alpha'}(h(\mathrm{Z}^q))\).

(iii) \(f(\exp(\mathrm{Y})) = \exp(h^\vee(\mathrm{Y}^q))\).

(Each condition should be read: for every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and every \(z \in \mathbb{G}_m(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{Z} \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^*(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{Y} \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})(\mathrm{S}')\), we have …).
Indeed, (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii) by 3.8, (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii) by 3.7, (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i) by 2.7.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.11

Let S be a prescheme, \(q \in \mathbb{Z}\), \(q > 0\), such that \(x \mapsto x^q\) defines an endomorphism of \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\), \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) and \((\mathrm{G}', \mathrm{T}', \alpha')\) two S-elementary systems, and \(f : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}'\) a morphism of groups. The following conditions on \(f\) are equivalent:

(i) The restriction of \(f\) to T factors through a morphism \(f_\mathrm{T} : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{T}'\) making the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \ar[r]^-{\alpha^*} \ar[d]_-{q} & \mathrm{T} \ar[r]^-{\alpha} \ar[d]^-{f_\mathrm{T}} & \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \ar[d]^-{q} \\ \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \ar[r]_-{\alpha'^*} & \mathrm{T}' \ar[r]_-{\alpha'} & \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}. } \] commute.

(ii) There exists a (unique) isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Modules \[ h : (\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^{\otimes q} \longrightarrow \mathfrak{g}'^{\alpha'} \] such that \(f(\exp(\mathrm{X})) = \exp(h(\mathrm{X}^q))\), \(f(\exp(\mathrm{Y})) = \exp(h^\vee(\mathrm{Y}^q))\) for all \(\mathrm{X} \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{Y} \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) (it follows that \(f\) also satisfies the equivalent conditions of 3.10).

We have (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i). Indeed, by 3.10, (ii) implies \(f \circ \alpha^* = q\, \alpha'^*\); hence, by 3.3, \(f|\mathrm{T}\) factors through \(\mathrm{T}'\). It remains to prove \(\alpha'(f(t)) = \alpha(t)^q\), which follows at once from the fact that \(f\) induces a group morphism \(\mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{U}_\alpha \to \mathrm{T}' \cdot \mathrm{U}_{\alpha'}\). Let us prove (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii). Let \(\mathrm{X} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^\alpha)\), \(\mathrm{Y} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})\). Set \(p_+(x) = f(\exp(x\mathrm{X}))\), \(p_-(x) = f(\exp(y\mathrm{Y}))\); these are group morphisms \[ p_+, p_- : \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}. \] Now we have \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{int}(\alpha'^*(z))^q(p_+(x)) &= \mathrm{int}\big(f_\mathrm{T}(\alpha^*(z))\big)\big(f(\exp(x\mathrm{X}))\big) \\ &= f\big(\mathrm{int}(\alpha^*(z))(\exp(x\mathrm{X}))\big) = f(\exp(z^2 x\mathrm{X})) = p_+(z^2 x). \end{aligned} \] Applying Lemma 1.2 (with \(\mathrm{Q} = \alpha'^*(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}})\)), we deduce that there exists a section \(\mathrm{X}' \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}'^{\alpha'})\) with \[ f(\exp(x\mathrm{X})) = p_+(x) = \exp(x^q \mathrm{X}'). \] Likewise, there exists a section \(\mathrm{Y}' \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}'^{-\alpha'})\) with \[ f(\exp(y\mathrm{Y})) = \exp(y^q \mathrm{Y}'). \] Now writing that \(f\) is a group morphism, hence that it respects formula (F), we immediately obtain \[ \mathrm{X}^q \mathrm{Y}^q = (\mathrm{XY})^q = \mathrm{X}'\mathrm{Y}'. \] One easily concludes that \(\mathrm{X}^q \mapsto \mathrm{X}'\) and \(\mathrm{Y}^q \mapsto \mathrm{Y}'\) define isomorphisms \(h\) and \(h^\vee\) as announced.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.12

Let \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) be an S-elementary system and \(w \in \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{S})\); set \[ \Omega_0 = \Omega \cap \mathrm{int}(w^{-1})(\Omega). \] Let \(d\) be the function on \(\Omega\) defined by \[ d(\exp(\mathrm{Y}) \cdot t \cdot \exp(\mathrm{X})) = \alpha(t)^{-1} + \mathrm{XY}. \] Then \(\Omega_0 = \Omega_d\) and for \(\exp(\mathrm{Y}) \cdot t \cdot \exp(\mathrm{X}) \in \Omega_0(\mathrm{S}')\) we have the following formula (we set \(d = d(\exp(\mathrm{Y}) \cdot t \cdot \exp(\mathrm{X}))\)): \[ \mathrm{int}(w)(\exp(\mathrm{Y}) \cdot t \cdot \exp(\mathrm{X})) = \exp(d^{-1} a_\alpha(w)^{-1}\mathrm{X}) \cdot t\, \alpha^*(d) \cdot \exp(d^{-1} a_\alpha(w)\mathrm{Y}). \] Moreover, \(d \circ \mathrm{int}(w) = d^{-1}\).

Indeed, we have at once \[ \mathrm{int}(w)(\exp(\mathrm{Y}) \cdot t \cdot \exp(\mathrm{X})) = \exp(a_\alpha(w)^{-1}\mathrm{Y}) \cdot t\alpha^*(\alpha(t))^{-1} \cdot \exp(a_\alpha(w)\mathrm{X}). \] By (2.4), this is a section of \(\Omega\) if and only if \(\alpha(t)\mathrm{XY} + 1\) is invertible, which indeed proves \(\Omega_0 = \Omega_d\); then applying formula (F), we deduce the announced formula by a trivial computation. The last assertion is then immediate.
N. B. Note that the function \(d\) is independent of the choice of \(w\).
4. The isomorphism theorem

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.1

Let S be a prescheme, \(q \in \mathbb{Z}\), \(q > 0\) such that \(x \mapsto x^q\) is an endomorphism of \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\), and \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) and \((\mathrm{G}', \mathrm{T}', \alpha')\) two S-elementary systems. Let \[ h : (\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^{\otimes q} \longrightarrow \mathfrak{g}'^{\alpha'} \] and \[ h^\vee : (\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})^{\otimes q} \longrightarrow \mathfrak{g}'^{-\alpha'} \] be two isomorphisms, contragredient to each other. Let \(f_\mathrm{T} : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{T}'\) be a morphism of S-groups making the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \ar[r]^-{q} \ar[d]_-{\alpha^*} & \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \ar[d]^-{\alpha'^*} \\ \mathrm{T} \ar[r]^-{f_\mathrm{T}} \ar[d]_-{\alpha} & \mathrm{T}' \ar[d]^-{\alpha'} \\ \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \ar[r]_-{q} & \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}. } \] commute. There exists a unique morphism of S-groups \(f : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}'\) which extends \(f_\mathrm{T}\) and satisfies \[ f(\exp(\mathrm{X})) = \exp(h(\mathrm{X}^q)) \] for every \(\mathrm{X} \in \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha)(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\). Moreover, this morphism also satisfies \[ f(\exp(\mathrm{Y})) = \exp(h^\vee(\mathrm{Y}^q)) \qquad \text{and} \qquad f(w_\alpha(\mathrm{Z})) = w_{\alpha'}(h(\mathrm{Z}^q)), \] for every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and all \(\mathrm{Y} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha})\), \(\mathrm{Z} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathfrak{g}^\alpha)^*\).

If \(f : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}'\) extends \(f_\mathrm{T}\), then \(f \circ \alpha^* = (\alpha'^*)^q\). If moreover \(f\) satisfies the second condition, then it also satisfies the other two by 3.10. It follows that \(f\) is determined on \(\Omega\) by the relation \[ f(\exp(\mathrm{Y})\, t \exp(\mathrm{X})) = \exp(h^\vee(\mathrm{Y}^q)) f_\mathrm{T}(t) \exp(h(\mathrm{X}^q)). \] Since \(\Omega\) is schematically dense in G, this already proves the uniqueness of \(f\). To prove its existence, it suffices, by virtue of Exp. XVIII 2.3, to prove that the preceding formula defines a "generically multiplicative" morphism from \(\Omega\) to \(\mathrm{G}'\). Now, by 2.4, this amounts to checking that \(\alpha' \circ f = \alpha^q\), which follows from the fact that \(f\) extends \(f_\mathrm{T}\).

blocks.title: Scholium
blocks.html:
Scholium 4.2

4.1 can also be interpreted in the following way: consider the category \(\mathscr{E}\) of S-elementary systems and the category \(\mathscr{D}\) of pairs \[ \left( \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \xrightarrow{\ \alpha^*\ } \mathrm{T} \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}},\ \mathscr{L} \right), \] where T is a torus, \(\alpha\) and \(\alpha^*\) group morphisms such that \(\alpha \circ \alpha^* = 2\), and \(\mathscr{L}\) an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module (the reader will make precise the morphisms of the two categories considered). One defines a functor \(\mathscr{E} \to \mathscr{D}\) by \[ (\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha) \longmapsto \left( \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \xrightarrow{\ \alpha^*\ } \mathrm{T} \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}},\ \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \right). \] The preceding theorem says that this functor is fully faithful. It is in fact an equivalence of categories, as will be seen in the next section. We already have:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3

If \(q = 1\) and if \(f_\mathrm{T}\) is an isomorphism, then \(f\) is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4

If \(q = 1\) and if \(f_\mathrm{T}\) is faithfully flat with kernel Q (cf. Exp. IX 2.7), then \(f\) is faithfully flat (quasi-compact) with kernel Q, hence identifies \(\mathrm{G}'\) with \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Q}\).

Indeed, if \(f_\mathrm{T}\) is faithfully flat with kernel Q, then \[ \mathrm{Q} = \mathrm{Ker}(f_\mathrm{T}) \subset \mathrm{Ker}(f_\mathrm{T} \circ \alpha') = \mathrm{Ker}(\alpha). \] Introducing the S-elementary system \((\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{T}/\mathrm{Q}, \alpha/\mathrm{Q})\) of 2.13, we are reduced by 2.14 to proving that \(f/\mathrm{Q}\) induces an isomorphism from \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Q}\) onto \(\mathrm{G}'\), which follows at once from 4.3.
5. Examples of elementary systems, applications

Let S be a prescheme and \(\mathscr{L}\) an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module.
