Consider the group \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}}\) over S defined by \[ \mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}}(\mathrm{S}') = \left\{ \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix} \ \middle|\ a, d \in \mathbb{G}_a(\mathrm{S}'), \ \begin{array}{l} b \in \mathrm{W}(\mathscr{L})(\mathrm{S}') \\ c \in \mathrm{W}(\mathscr{L}^{-1})(\mathrm{S}') \end{array}, \ ad - bc \in \mathbb{G}_m(\mathrm{S}') \right\} \] endowed with the usual law of matrix multiplication. It is locally isomorphic to \(\mathrm{GL}_{2,\mathrm{S}}\). It is therefore an S-group scheme, affine and smooth over S, with connected fibers.

Remark. — Let \(\mathscr{L}'\) and \(\mathscr{L}''\) be two invertible sheaves on S such that \(\mathscr{L} = \mathscr{L}'' \otimes \mathscr{L}'^{-1}\). Then, by the formulas \((x, y) \mapsto (ax + by, cx + dy)\), one realizes an isomorphism of S-groups \[ \mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{GL}(\mathscr{L}' \oplus \mathscr{L}''). \]

We will denote by \(\mathrm{S}_{\mathscr{L}}\) the closed subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}}\) defined by the relation \(ad - bc = 1\). It is likewise an S-group scheme, affine and smooth over S, with connected fibers (isomorphic to \(\mathrm{SL}(\mathscr{L}' \oplus \mathscr{L}'')\) via the preceding isomorphism).

Similarly, consider the morphism \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}}\) defined by \(z \mapsto \left(\begin{smallmatrix} z & 0 \\ 0 & z \end{smallmatrix}\right)\). It is a central monomorphism; by passing to the quotient, we deduce from it a group \(\mathrm{P}_{\mathscr{L}}\), smooth and affine over S, with connected fibers (cf. Exp. VIII 5.7).

One can see that, by passing to the quotient from the isomorphism of the remark, \(\mathrm{P}_{\mathscr{L}}\) is identified with the group of automorphisms of the projective bundle \(\mathbb{P}(\mathscr{L}' \oplus \mathscr{L}'')\) (cf. EGA, II 4.2.7). We will denote by \(i\) and \(p\) the canonical morphisms \[ \mathrm{S}_{\mathscr{L}} \xrightarrow{\ i\ } \mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}} \xrightarrow{\ p\ } \mathrm{P}_{\mathscr{L}}\,; \] \(i\) is a closed immersion, \(p\) is faithfully flat and affine.

Consider the group morphisms \[ t_\mathrm{G} : \mathbb{G}^2_{m,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}}, \qquad t_\mathrm{G}(z, z') = \begin{pmatrix} z & 0 \\ 0 & z' \end{pmatrix}; \] \[ t_\mathrm{S} : \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{S}_{\mathscr{L}}, \qquad t_\mathrm{S}(z) = \begin{pmatrix} z & 0 \\ 0 & z^{-1} \end{pmatrix}; \] \[ t_\mathrm{P} : \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{P}_{\mathscr{L}}, \qquad t_\mathrm{P}(z) = p(t_\mathrm{G}(z, 1)). \] These are group monomorphisms, which define in each group a (trivial) torus of relative codimension 2. For every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), let \[ \mathrm{X} \in \Gamma(\bar{s}, \mathscr{L} \otimes \bar{s})^*\,; \] then the section \(\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 0 & \mathrm{X} \\ \mathrm{X}^{-1} & 0 \end{smallmatrix}\right)\) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}, \bar{s}}\) normalizes \(t_\mathrm{G}(\mathbb{G}^2_{m,\bar{s}})\) and does not centralize it; we then conclude from Exp. XIX 1.6 that \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}}\) is reductive, of semisimple rank 1, with maximal torus \(t_\mathrm{G}(\mathbb{G}^2_{m,\mathrm{S}})\).

One argues likewise for \(\mathrm{S}_{\mathscr{L}}\) and \(\mathrm{P}_{\mathscr{L}}\), and one sees that \(\mathrm{S}_{\mathscr{L}}\) (resp. \(\mathrm{P}_{\mathscr{L}}\)) is reductive, of semisimple rank 1, with maximal torus \(t_\mathrm{S}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}})\) (resp. \(t_\mathrm{P}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}})\)).

Arguing as usual, one immediately determines the Lie Algebra of these various groups and the adjoint action of the chosen maximal torus. Let us do it for \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}}\); it is immediate by Exp. II 4.8: \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}}/\mathrm{S})\) is the Lie Algebra of matrices \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}}/\mathrm{S}) = \left\{ \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix} \ \middle|\ a \text{ and } d \text{ sections of } \mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S},\ b \text{ a section of } \mathscr{L},\ c \text{ a section of } \mathscr{L}^{-1} \right\} \] with the usual bracket; we have \[ \mathrm{Ad}(t_\mathrm{G}(z, z')) \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} a & zz'^{-1}b \\ z'z^{-1}c & d \end{pmatrix}. \] Let us write \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}}/\mathrm{S}) = \mathfrak{g}\). Let \(\alpha_\mathrm{G} : t_\mathrm{G}(\mathbb{G}^2_{m,\mathrm{S}}) \to \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\) be the character defined by \[ \alpha_\mathrm{G}(t_\mathrm{G}(z, z')) = zz'^{-1}. \] One sees at once from the preceding relation that \(\alpha_\mathrm{G}\) is a root of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}}\) with respect to \(t_\mathrm{G}(\mathbb{G}^2_{m,\mathrm{S}})\) and that the morphism \[ u : \mathscr{L} \longrightarrow \mathfrak{g} \qquad (\text{resp. } u_- : \mathscr{L}^{-1} \longrightarrow \mathfrak{g}) \] defined by \(u(\mathrm{X}) = \left(\begin{smallmatrix} 0 & \mathrm{X} \\ 0 & 0 \end{smallmatrix}\right)\) (resp. \(u_-(\mathrm{X}) = \left(\begin{smallmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ \mathrm{X} & 0 \end{smallmatrix}\right)\)) is an isomorphism of \(\mathscr{L}\) onto \(\mathfrak{g}^{\alpha_\mathrm{G}}\) (resp. of \(\mathscr{L}^{-1}\) onto \(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha_\mathrm{G}}\)).
We have thus proved that \((\mathrm{G}, t_\mathrm{G}(\mathbb{G}^2_{m,\mathrm{S}}), \alpha_\mathrm{G})\) is an S-elementary system.

Setting likewise \[ \alpha_\mathrm{S}(t_\mathrm{S}(z)) = z^2, \qquad \alpha_\mathrm{P}(t_\mathrm{P}(z)) = z, \] one proves that \((\mathrm{S}_{\mathscr{L}}, t_\mathrm{S}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}), \alpha_\mathrm{S})\) and \((\mathrm{P}_{\mathscr{L}}, t_\mathrm{P}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}), \alpha_\mathrm{P})\) are elementary systems, and one defines isomorphisms of \(\mathscr{L}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{L}^{-1}\)) with the corresponding direct factors of the Lie Algebras of \(\mathrm{S}_{\mathscr{L}}\) and \(\mathrm{P}_{\mathscr{L}}\).

Set \(\exp\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 0 & \mathrm{X} \\ 0 & 0 \end{smallmatrix}\right) = \left(\begin{smallmatrix} 1 & \mathrm{X} \\ 0 & 1 \end{smallmatrix}\right)\). We have thus defined a morphism \[ \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}^{\alpha_\mathrm{G}}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}} \] which induces on Lie Algebras the canonical morphism, hence is the unique morphism of this type (1.5). Likewise, we set \(\exp\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ \mathrm{Y} & 0 \end{smallmatrix}\right) = \left(\begin{smallmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ \mathrm{Y} & 1 \end{smallmatrix}\right)\). Carrying out the explicit computation of formula (F), one finds \[ \left\langle \begin{pmatrix} 0 & \mathrm{X} \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ \mathrm{Y} & 0 \end{pmatrix} \right\rangle = \mathrm{XY}, \qquad \alpha_\mathrm{G}^*(z) = \begin{pmatrix} z & 0 \\ 0 & z^{-1} \end{pmatrix} = t_\mathrm{G}(z, z^{-1}). \] The morphism \(w_{\alpha_\mathrm{G}}\) is then \[ w_{\alpha_\mathrm{G}} \begin{pmatrix} 0 & \mathrm{X} \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & \mathrm{X} \\ -\mathrm{X}^{-1} & 0 \end{pmatrix}; \] the morphism \(a_{\alpha_\mathrm{G}}\) is \[ a_{\alpha_\mathrm{G}} \begin{pmatrix} 0 & \mathrm{P} \\ \mathrm{Q} & 0 \end{pmatrix} = \mathrm{PQ}^{-1}. \]

We leave it to the reader to carry out the same computations in \(\mathrm{S}_{\mathscr{L}}\) and \(\mathrm{P}_{\mathscr{L}}\). One finds the same duality formula and the coroots \[ \alpha_\mathrm{S}^*(z) = t_\mathrm{S}(z), \qquad \alpha_\mathrm{P}^*(z) = t_\mathrm{P}(z^2). \] Denoting by \(p_\mathrm{T}\) the morphism induced by \(p : \mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}} \to \mathrm{P}_{\mathscr{L}}\) on \(t_\mathrm{S}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}})\), namely \(p_\mathrm{T}(t_\mathrm{S}(z)) = t_\mathrm{P}(z^2)\), we therefore have the commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix@R=2.4em@C=1.1em{ & & \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} & & \\ \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \ar[r]_-{t_\mathrm{S}} \ar[urr]^-{2} \ar[drr]_-{\mathrm{id}} & t_\mathrm{S}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}) \ar[ur]_-{\alpha_\mathrm{S}} \ar[dr]^-{\alpha_\mathrm{S}^*} \ar[rr]_-{p_\mathrm{T}} & & t_\mathrm{P}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}) \ar[ul]^-{\alpha_\mathrm{P}} \ar[dl]_-{\alpha_\mathrm{P}^*} & \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \ar[l]^-{t_\mathrm{P}} \ar[ull]_-{\mathrm{id}} \ar[dll]^-{2} \\ & & \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} & & } \] In the central part one recognizes the commutative diagram of 4.1 relative to the canonical morphism \(p \circ i : \mathrm{S}_{\mathscr{L}} \to \mathrm{P}_{\mathscr{L}}\), which induces a morphism of the preceding S-elementary systems.

Now let \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) be an arbitrary elementary system. Consider the commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix@R=2em@C=1.4em{ & \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \ar[dl]_-{\mathrm{id}} \ar[d]^-{\alpha^*} \ar[dr]^-{2} & \\ \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \ar[r]_-{\alpha^*} \ar[dr]_-{2} & \mathrm{T} \ar[r]_-{\alpha} \ar[d]^-{\alpha} & \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \ar[dl]^-{\mathrm{id}} \\ & \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} & } \] Combining the two preceding diagrams, we immediately obtain a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix@R=2em@C=2.4em{ & \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \ar[dl]_-{\alpha_\mathrm{S}^*} \ar[d]^-{\alpha^*} \ar[dr]^-{\alpha_\mathrm{P}^*} & \\ t_\mathrm{S}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}) \ar[r]_-{\alpha^* \circ t_\mathrm{S}^{-1}} \ar[dr]_-{\alpha_\mathrm{S}} & \mathrm{T} \ar[r]_-{t_\mathrm{P} \circ \alpha} \ar[d]^-{\alpha} & t_\mathrm{P}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}) \ar[dl]^-{\alpha_\mathrm{P}} \\ & \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} & } \] Using 4.1, we therefore have:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.8

Let S be a prescheme and \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) an S-elementary system. Set \(\mathscr{L} = \mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) (and hence \(\mathscr{L}^{-1} = \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\)).

(i) There exists a unique group morphism \(f : \mathrm{S}_{\mathscr{L}} \to \mathrm{G}\) which satisfies the following equivalent conditions:

(a) \(f\left(\begin{smallmatrix} z & 0 \\ 0 & z^{-1} \end{smallmatrix}\right) = \alpha^*(z)\), \(\quad f\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 1 & \mathrm{X} \\ 0 & 1 \end{smallmatrix}\right) = \exp(\mathrm{X})\);

(b) \(f\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 1 & \mathrm{X} \\ 0 & 1 \end{smallmatrix}\right) = \exp(\mathrm{X})\), \(\quad f\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ \mathrm{Y} & 1 \end{smallmatrix}\right) = \exp(\mathrm{Y})\);

(c) \(f\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 1 & \mathrm{X} \\ 0 & 1 \end{smallmatrix}\right) = \exp(\mathrm{X})\), \(\quad f\left(\begin{smallmatrix} 0 & \mathrm{X} \\ -\mathrm{X}^{-1} & 0 \end{smallmatrix}\right) = w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})\).

(ii) There exists a unique group morphism \(g : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{P}_{\mathscr{L}}\) which satisfies \[ g(t) = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha(t) & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad g(\exp(\mathrm{X})) = p\begin{pmatrix} 1 & \mathrm{X} \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}. \] Moreover, we have \[ g(\exp(\mathrm{Y})) = p\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ \mathrm{Y} & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad g(w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})) = p\begin{pmatrix} 0 & \mathrm{X} \\ -\mathrm{X}^{-1} & 0 \end{pmatrix}. \] The morphism \(g\) is faithfully flat quasi-compact with kernel \(\mathrm{Ker}(\alpha) = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(\mathrm{G})\), and \(g \circ f\) is the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{S}_{\mathscr{L}} \to \mathrm{P}_{\mathscr{L}}\).
Note that conditions (b) of (i) give an explicit description of the duality between \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) and \(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.9

Let \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) be an S-elementary system. The subgroups \(\mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{U}_\alpha\), \(\mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}\), \(\mathrm{U}_\alpha\) and \(\mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}\) are closed.

Since \(\mathrm{U}_\alpha\) is a closed subgroup prescheme of \(\mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{U}_\alpha\), it suffices to carry out the verification for the latter. By Noether's theorem (Exp. IV 5.3.1 and 6.4.1), it suffices to prove that \((\mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{U}_\alpha)/\mathrm{Ker}(\alpha)\) is a closed subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Ker}(\alpha)\). By virtue of 5.8, we are thus reduced to proving that the subgroup of \(\mathrm{P}_{\mathscr{L}}\) (or of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}}\), which amounts to the same thing by virtue of a further application of Noether's theorem) defined by \(c = 0\) is closed, which is trivial.
In other words, the exp morphisms of 1.5 are closed immersions.
N. B. This also follows from the fact that \(\mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{U}_\alpha\) and \(\mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}\) are "Borel groups" of G (cf. Exp. XII 7.10).

Let \(\mathscr{L}\) be an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-Module and \[ \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \xrightarrow{\ \alpha^*\ } \mathrm{T} \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \] a diagram of groups such that \(\alpha \circ \alpha^* = 2\). Let R be the maximal torus of \(\mathrm{Ker}(\alpha)\) and \(\mathrm{K} = \alpha^{*-1}(\mathrm{R})\). It is a subgroup of multiplicative type of \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\); by virtue of \(\alpha \circ \alpha^* = 2\), it is even a subgroup of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{2,\mathrm{S}}\). In particular, the morphism \[ \mathrm{K} \longrightarrow \mathrm{S}_{\mathscr{L}}, \qquad z \mapsto \begin{pmatrix} z & 0 \\ 0 & z^{-1} \end{pmatrix} \] is central. We therefore have a central group monomorphism: \[ \mathrm{K} \longrightarrow \mathrm{R} \times \mathrm{S}_{\mathscr{L}} \] defined by \(k \mapsto \left(\alpha^*(k),\, \left(\begin{smallmatrix} k & 0 \\ 0 & k^{-1} \end{smallmatrix}\right)\right)\). Consider the group G obtained by passing to the quotient. It is a group, affine and smooth over S, with connected fibers. It is immediate that the sequence \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{K} \longrightarrow \mathrm{R} \times t_\mathrm{S}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}) \xrightarrow{\ u\ } \mathrm{T} \longrightarrow 1 \] where \(u(x, t_\mathrm{S}(z)) = x\, \alpha^*(z)\) is exact. The image of \(\mathrm{R} \times t_\mathrm{S}(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}})\) in G is therefore a torus \(\mathrm{T}'\) isomorphic to T. One now shows without difficulty that if \(\alpha'\) is the character of \(\mathrm{T}'\) deduced from \(\alpha\) by the preceding isomorphism, then \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}', \alpha')\) is an S-elementary system, that \(\mathfrak{g}^{\alpha'}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathscr{L}\), and that \(\alpha'^*\) is obtained from \(\alpha^*\) by the isomorphism \(\mathrm{T} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{T}'\). We have thus constructed an S-elementary system \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}', \alpha')\) such that the corresponding object \(\left(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \xrightarrow{\alpha'^*} \mathrm{T}' \xrightarrow{\alpha'} \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}},\ \mathfrak{g}^{\alpha'}\right)\) of the category defined in 4.2 is isomorphic to \(\left(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \xrightarrow{\alpha^*} \mathrm{T} \xrightarrow{\alpha} \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}},\ \mathscr{L}\right)\). We have therefore proved the

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.11

In the notation of 4.2, the functor \[ (\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha) \longmapsto \left( \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \xrightarrow{\ \alpha^*\ } \mathrm{T} \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}},\ \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \right) \] is an equivalence of categories.

pages.title: 6. Generators and relations for an elementary system

blocks.html: 6. Generators and relations for an elementary system

Let S be a prescheme and \((\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{T}, \alpha)\) an S-elementary system. Let \(u \in \mathrm{U}_\alpha^*(\mathrm{S})\); we saw in 3.8 that the corresponding element \(w = w_\alpha(\mathrm{X})\) (with \(u = \exp(\mathrm{X})\)) satisfies in particular the relation \[ (w\, u)^3 = e. \]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.2

Let H be an S-sheaf of groups for (fppf). Let \[ f_\mathrm{T} : \mathrm{T} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}, \qquad f_\alpha : \mathrm{U}_\alpha \longrightarrow \mathrm{H} \] be group morphisms and \(h \in \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{S})\) a section of H. For there to exist a group morphism (necessarily unique) \[ f : \mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H} \] extending \(f_\mathrm{T}\) and \(f_\alpha\) and satisfying \(f(w) = h\), it is necessary and sufficient that the following conditions be satisfied:

(i) For every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\), every \(t \in \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S}')\) and every \(x \in \mathrm{U}_\alpha(\mathrm{S}')\), we have \[ (1) \qquad f_\mathrm{T}(t)\, f_\alpha(x)\, f_\mathrm{T}(t)^{-1} = f_\alpha(t\, x\, t^{-1}) = f_\alpha(x^{\alpha(t)}). \] (in other words, \(f_\mathrm{T}\) and \(f_\alpha\) extend to a group morphism from the semidirect product \(\mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{U}_\alpha\) to H).

(ii) For every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and every \(t \in \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S}')\), we have \[ (2) \qquad h\, f_\mathrm{T}(t)\, h^{-1} = f_\mathrm{T}(s_\alpha(t)) = f_\mathrm{T}(t \cdot \alpha^*(\alpha(t)^{-1})). \]

(iii) We have the two relations in \(\mathrm{H}(\mathrm{S})\): \[ (3) \qquad h^2 = f_\mathrm{T}(\alpha^*(-1)), \] \[ (4) \qquad (h\, f_\alpha(u))^3 = e. \]

Proof. Let us write \(\mathrm{U}_\alpha\) and \(\mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}\) additively and their vector structure multiplicatively. If \(f\) satisfies the conditions of the statement, we necessarily have, for every \(y \in \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}(\mathrm{S}')\), \[ f(y) = f(w^{-1}\, w\, y\, w^{-1}\, w) = h f_\alpha(w^{-1}\, y\, w) h^{-1}. \] So let \(f_{-\alpha} : \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha} \to \mathrm{H}\) be the morphism defined by \[ (*_1) \qquad f_{-\alpha}(y) = h f_\alpha(w^{-1}\, y\, w) h^{-1}. \] It is a group morphism. On the other hand, \(f\) is determined on the big cell \(\Omega\) by \[ f(y\, t\, x) = f_{-\alpha}(y) f_\mathrm{T}(t) f_\alpha(x). \] This shows the uniqueness of \(f\); since the conditions of the statement are manifestly necessary, let us show that they are sufficient. By (4) we have \[ h f_\alpha(u) h^{-1} h^2 = f_\alpha(-u) h^{-1} f_\alpha(-u). \] Now, by (3) and (i), \(h^2\) commutes with \(f_\alpha(-u)\), which gives \[ h f_\alpha(u) h^{-1} = f_\alpha(-u) h f_\alpha(-u). \] But, by definition, \(h f_\alpha(u) h^{-1} = f_{-\alpha}(w\, u\, w^{-1})\); since \(w\) corresponds to \(u\), we have \(w\, u\, w^{-1} = -\widetilde{u}\) (we denote by \(\widetilde{u} \in \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}^*(\mathrm{S})\) the element paired with \(u\), cf. 3.7); we thus obtain \[ f_{-\alpha}(-\widetilde{u}) = f_\alpha(-u) h f_\alpha(-u). \] Now let \(t\) be a section of T over a varying \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\). Let us apply \(\mathrm{int}(f_\mathrm{T}(t))\) to the preceding formula. On the left-hand side we obtain \[ \begin{aligned} f_\mathrm{T}(t) f_{-\alpha}(-\widetilde{u}) f_\mathrm{T}(t)^{-1} &= f_\mathrm{T}(t) h f_\alpha(u) h^{-1} f_\mathrm{T}(t) \\ &= h(h^{-1} f_\mathrm{T}(t) h) f_\alpha(u)(h^{-1} f_\mathrm{T}(t) h) h^{-1} \\ &= h f_\mathrm{T}(s_\mathrm{T}(t)) f_\alpha(u) f_\mathrm{T}(s_\alpha(t))^{-1} h^{-1}, \end{aligned} \] by (2) and (3); then \[ h f_\alpha(\alpha(s_\alpha(t))u) h^{-1} = h f_\alpha(\alpha(t)^{-1} u) h^{-1} \] by (1), and then finally, by construction, \(f_{-\alpha}(-\alpha(t)^{-1}\widetilde{u})\). The right-hand side gives \[ \begin{aligned} &f_\alpha(-\alpha(t)u) \cdot f_\mathrm{T}(t) h f_\mathrm{T}(t)^{-1} h^{-1} h \cdot f_\alpha(-\alpha(t)u) \\ &= f_\alpha(-\alpha(t)u) f_\mathrm{T}(t) f_\mathrm{T}(t^{-1} \alpha^*(\alpha(t))) h f_\alpha(-\alpha(t)u). \end{aligned} \] Comparing the two expressions obtained, we get \[ f_{-\alpha}(-\alpha(t)^{-1}\widetilde{u}) = f_\alpha(-\alpha(t)u) \cdot f_\mathrm{T}(\alpha^*(\alpha(t))) \cdot h \cdot f_\alpha(-\alpha(t)u). \] Since \(\alpha : \mathrm{T} \to \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\) is faithfully flat and H is a separated presheaf, we may replace \(-\alpha(t)^{-1}\) by an arbitrary section of \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\), and we obtain the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.2.1

For every \(z \in \mathbb{G}_m(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\), we have \[ f_{-\alpha}(z\widetilde{u}) = f_\alpha(z^{-1}u) \cdot f_\mathrm{T}\big(\alpha^*(-z^{-1})\big) \cdot h \cdot f_\alpha(z^{-1}u). \]

Now let \(x, y \in \mathbb{G}_a(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\), and suppose \(y\) and \((1 + xy)\) invertible. First applying the lemma to \(z = y\), we obtain \[ \begin{aligned} f_\alpha(xu) f_{-\alpha}(y\widetilde{u}) &= f_\alpha((x + y^{-1})u) \cdot f_\mathrm{T}(\alpha^*(-y^{-1})) \cdot h \cdot f_\alpha(y^{-1}u) \\ &= f_\alpha((x + y^{-1})u) \cdot f_\mathrm{T}\big(\alpha^*(-(x + y^{-1}))\big)\, f_\mathrm{T}\!\left( \alpha^*\!\left( \frac{y^{-1}}{x + y^{-1}} \right) \right) \cdot h \cdot f_\alpha(y^{-1}u). \end{aligned} \] Now \(x + y^{-1} = y^{-1}(1 + xy)\). Applying the lemma to \(z = \dfrac{y}{1 + xy}\), we find \[ \begin{aligned} f_\alpha(xu) f_{-\alpha}(y\widetilde{u}) &= f_{-\alpha}\!\left( \frac{y}{1 + xy}\widetilde{u} \right) \cdot f_\alpha(-(x + y^{-1})u) \cdot h^{-1} f_\mathrm{T}(\alpha^*(1 + xy)^{-1}) h \cdot f_\alpha(y^{-1}u) \\ &= f_{-\alpha}\!\left( \frac{y}{1 + xy}\widetilde{u} \right) \cdot f_\alpha(-(x + y^{-1})u) \cdot f_\mathrm{T}(\alpha^*(1 + xy)) \cdot f_\alpha(y^{-1}u) \\ &= f_{-\alpha}\!\left( \frac{y}{1 + xy}\widetilde{u} \right) \cdot f_\mathrm{T}(\alpha^*(1 + xy)) \cdot f_\alpha\big((-(x + y^{-1})(1 + xy)^{-2} + y^{-1})u\big) \\ &= f_{-\alpha}\!\left( \frac{y}{1 + xy}\widetilde{u} \right) \cdot f_\mathrm{T}(\alpha^*(1 + xy)) \cdot f_\alpha\!\left( \frac{x}{1 + xy}u \right). \end{aligned} \] We have thus proved:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.2.2

If \(a \in \mathrm{U}_\alpha(\mathrm{S}')\), \(b \in \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}^*(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) are such that \(1 + ab \in \mathbb{G}_m(\mathrm{S}')\), we have \[ f_\alpha(a) f_{-\alpha}(b) = f_{-\alpha}\!\left( \frac{b}{1 + ab} \right) f_\mathrm{T}\big(\alpha^*(1 + ab)\big) f_\alpha\!\left( \frac{a}{1 + ab} \right). \]

By schematic density, this formula remains valid when \(b \in \mathrm{U}_{-\alpha}(\mathrm{S}')\), with \(1 + ab\) still invertible. Then consider the morphism \[ f : \Omega \longrightarrow \mathrm{H} \] defined by \(f(y\, t\, x) = f_{-\alpha}(y) f_\mathrm{T}(t) f_\alpha(x)\). It follows at once from 6.2.2, from condition (i), and from formula (F′) of 2.4, that if \(g, g' \in \Omega(\mathrm{S}')\) and \(gg' \in \Omega(\mathrm{S}')\), then \(f(gg') = f(g)f(g')\). By Exp. XVIII 2.3 (iii) and 2.4, there therefore exists a group morphism \(\mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) extending \(f\). Let us also denote it \(f\); it answers the question; indeed, it suffices to prove that \(f(w_\alpha) = h\). Now \(w_\alpha = u \cdot (-\widetilde{u}) \cdot u\), whence \[ \begin{aligned} f(w_\alpha) &= f_\alpha(u) f_{-\alpha}(-\widetilde{u}) f_\alpha(u) = f_\alpha(u) h f_\alpha(u) h^{-1} f_\alpha(u) \\ &= f_\alpha(u) h f_\alpha(u) h f_\alpha(u) h^2 = (f_\alpha(u)h)^3 h = h. \end{aligned} \]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 6.3

We will complete these results in Exp. XXIII 3.5.
